Major Surgical or Invasive Procedure:
___: Diagnostic angiogram of bilateral lower extremities 
via left groin access 

 
History of Present Illness:
Ms. ___ is a ___ female who returns to the Vascular 
Surgery Clinic for evaluation of her
peripheral vascular disease.  She is a primary patient of Dr. 
___. The patient has a history of a left femoral to 
posterior tibial bypass graft completed in ___.  It was done 
in the setting of a non-healing toe amp site and left ___ toe 
ulcer. When she was here in ___, ultrasound demonstrated 
that the left SFA and the bypass graft were occluded.  Since her 
toe amp site had healed and ___ toe ulcer improved it was 
determined that a redo bypass was not necessary at that time. 
The patient was instructed to continue to follow up with 
Podiatry to carefully watch the fourth toe ulceration.  The 
patient has been following with Podiatry on a monthly basis. 

Today, she returns to clinic with a new left second toe 
ulceration as well as a left first hallux blister with drainage, 
in addition to her non-healing ___ toe ulcer.  She also reports 
in the last couple of days, she has developed a new ulceration 
on her right fourth toe.  She has been covering these ulcers per 
Podiatry, with Adaptic dressing and Kerlix.  She endorses daily 
statin and aspirin as well as, gabapentin and Tylenol for her
rest pain and toe pain. She denies fevers, chills and body 
aches. She reports difficulty with ambulation due to her toe 
pain.
 
Past Medical History:
1) Ovarian cancer (Borderline Ovarian Mucinous Tumor) status

Brief Hospital Course:
Patient is a ___ year old woman with PMH of left femoral to ___ 
bypass (___) for a non-healing second toe amputation site and 
___ toe ulcer. During admission with Podiatry for a third toe 
amputation in ___, ultrasound demonstrated that the left SFA 
and the bypass graft were occluded, but since her second toe amp 
site had healed and ___ toe ulcer improved it was decided that 
redo bypass would not be attempted at that time. Upon returning 
to clinic on ___, the patient was found to have a new left 
second toe ulceration as well as a left first hallux blister 
with drainage, in addition to her non-healing ___ toe ulcer, and 
that pain from these wounds was limiting her ambulation. It was 
decided she would ultimately require a revision of her bypass 
graft after a bilateral lower extremity angiogram. 

The patient presented to ___ on ___ for a bilateral lower 
extremity angiogram. Procedure was uncomplicated, and 
demonstrated occlusion of the left SFA, bypass graft, and 
popliteal artery. It also demonstrated occlusion of the right 
SFA and popliteal artery. The patient went to PACU 
post-procedure in good condition, and after a brief, uneventful 
stay was transported to the floor. She was admitted to the 
Vascular Surgery service overnight for monitoring. 

On the morning of POD 1, the patient was doing well, afebrile 
with stable vital signs. The patient was tolerating a regular 
diet, ambulating, voiding without assistance, and pain was well 
controlled. Her groin access site was soft, with no evidence of 
bleeding or hematoma. She had dopplerable DP and ___ pulses in 
her right foot, and a dopplerable ___ pulse in her left foot. No 
DP pulse could be obtained on her left foot, which was unchanged 
from immediately post-procedure. The patient was discharged home 
without services. The patient unfortunately eloped from the 
hospital as her discharge papers were being completed, so she 
did not receive formal discharge teaching with nursing. Her 
nurse was notified the patient was eloping, and the patient 
allowed her nurse to remove her ___ in the hallway. The patient 
will return to the hospital on ___ for a 
planned readmission for left re-do left femoral to posterior 
tibial bypass.  

 
Medications on Admission:
The Preadmission Medication list is accurate and complete.
1. Acetaminophen 1000 mg PO Q6H:PRN pain 
2. Lisinopril 20 mg PO DAILY 
3. Gabapentin 400 mg PO TID 
4. Mirtazapine 45 mg PO QHS 
5. Rivaroxaban 2.5 mg PO BID 
6. Atorvastatin 80 mg PO QPM 
7. OxyCODONE (Immediate Release) 2.5 mg PO DAILY

9. Aspirin 81 mg PO DAILY 

 
Discharge Medications:
1.  Acetaminophen 1000 mg PO Q6H:PRN pain  
2.  Aspirin 81 mg PO DAILY  
3.  Atorvastatin 80 mg PO QPM  
4.  Gabapentin 400 mg PO TID  
5.  Mirtazapine 45 mg PO QHS  
6.  Omeprazole 20 mg PO BID  
7.  OxyCODONE (Immediate Release) 2.5 mg PO DAILY  
8. HELD- Lisinopril 20 mg PO DAILY  This medication was held. Do 
not restart Lisinopril until planned readmission ___
9. HELD- Rivaroxaban 2.5 mg PO BID  This medication was held. Do 
not restart Rivaroxaban until after planned readmission ___ 
___

 
Discharge Disposition:
Home
 
Discharge Diagnosis:
Peripheral vascular disease 
Toe ulcers

 
Discharge Condition:
Mental Status: Clear and coherent.
Level of Consciousness: Alert and interactive.
Activity Status: Ambulatory - requires assistance or aid (walker 
or cane).

 
Discharge Instructions:
Dear Ms. ___, 

It was a pleasure taking care of you at ___ 
___. You were admitted to the hospital after a 
peripheral angiogram. To do the test, a small puncture was made 
in one of your arteries. The puncture site heals on its own: 
there are no stitches to remove. You tolerated the procedure 
well and are now ready to be discharged from the hospital. You 
will return to the hospital on ___ for a 
bypass procedure on your leg. Please follow the recommendations 
below to ensure a speedy and uneventful recovery.

Peripheral Angiography

Puncture Site Care
For one week:
 Do not take a tub bath, go swimming or use a Jacuzzi or hot 
tub.
 Use only mild soap and water to gently clean the area around 
the puncture site. 
 Gently pat the puncture site dry after showering. 
 Do not use powders, lotions, or ointments in the area of the 
puncture site. 

You may remove the bandage and shower the day after the 
procedure.  You may leave the bandage off. 
You may have a small bruise around the puncture site.  This is 
normal and will go away one-two weeks. 

Activity 
For the first 48 hours: 
 Do not drive for 48 hours after the procedure
For the first week:
 Do not lift, push , pull or carry anything heavier than 10 
pounds
 Do not do any exercises or activity that causes you to hold 
your breath or bear down with abdominal muscles.   Take care not 
to put strain on your abdominal muscles when coughing, sneezing, 
or moving your bowels.

After one week:
 You may go back to all your regular activities, including 
sexual activity.  We suggest you begin your exercise program at 
half of your usual routine for the first few days.   You may 
then gradually work back to your full routine.

Medications:
Before you leave the hospital, you will be given a list of all 
the medicine you should take at home. If a medication that you 
normally take is not on the list or a medication that you do not 
take is on the list please discuss it with the team!  

For  Problems or Questions:
Call ___ in an emergency such as:
 Sudden, brisk bleeding or swelling at the groin puncture site 
that does not stop after applying pressure for ___ minutes
 Bleeding that is associated with nausea, weakness, or 
fainting.

Call the vascular surgery office (___) right away if 
you have any of the following. (Please note that someone is 
available 24 hours a day, 7 days a week)
 Swelling, bleeding, drainage, or discomfort at the puncture 
site that is new or increasing since discharge from the hospital
 Any change in sensation or temperature in your legs
 Fever of 101 or greater
 Any questions or concerns about recovery from your angiogram 

 
Followup Instructions:
___
 

Output:
answer:
The patient is a 54-year-old female who was admitted to the hospital for evaluation of her peripheral vascular disease and toe ulcers. She has a history of left femoral to posterior tibial bypass graft completed in the past, and has been experiencing toe pain and difficulty with ambulation. The patient has a history of ovarian cancer, hypertension, and other medical conditions. She is allergic to penicillins and NSAIDs.

The patient underwent a diagnostic angiogram of her bilateral lower extremities, which showed occlusion of the left SFA, bypass graft, and popliteal artery, as well as occlusion of the right SFA and popliteal artery. She was admitted to the hospital for monitoring and was discharged home without services.

The patient's medications at admission included acetaminophen, lisinopril, gabapentin, mirtazapine, rivaroxaban, atorvastatin, oxyCODONE, and omeprazole. Her medications at discharge included acetaminophen, aspirin, atorvastatin, gabapentin, mirtazapine, omeprazole, and oxyCODONE, with lisinopril and rivaroxaban held until her planned readmission.

The patient's discharge diagnosis was peripheral vascular disease and toe ulcers, and her discharge condition was clear and coherent, with a level of consciousness of alert and interactive, and activity status of ambulatory with assistance or aid.

___ Pathology 
DIAGNOSIS:  
1. Abdominal colon, colectomy (A-L): 
Chronic active colitis. Unremarkable ileum. 
2. Sigmoid colon, colectomy (M-X): 
Chronic active colitis.  
3. Omentum, resection (Y-AA): 
Unremarkable omentum. 
Note: Mucosal granulomas are present focally related to injured 
crypts. This finding is not specific and may be related to crypt 
rupture in any inflammatory condition, including ulcerative 
colitis. No fissures or transmural inflammation are seen. 

 
Brief Hospital Course:
Patient was brought to the pre-operative area, evaluated by 
anesthesia, and taken to the OR on ___ and underwent a 
total abdominal colectomy and end ileostomy as the small bowel 
was unable to be brought down for J-pouch (please refer to 
operative note for complete detail). Patient tolerated to the 
procedure well and was brought to the PACU for a short recovery 
before being brought to the floor.  

Neuro: Post operatively the patient received an epidural with 
good effect and adequate pain control of operative sight but had 
reports of shoulder pain and was started on IV tylenol.  Patient 
continued to report pain and was started on toradol on POD 1 for 
a 3 day coarse with good effect.  On POD1 patient reported some 
right leg motor weakness when attempting to ambulate and 
epidural was turned down by the Acute Pain Service with 
improvement of function, with full restoration after epidural 
was discontinued.  On POD 2 when tolerating oral intake, the 
patient was transitioned to PO oxycodone and tylenol with good 
effect.

CV: The patient remained stable from a cardiovascular 
standpoint; vital signs were routinely monitored.  Patient 
reported shoulder pain post-operatively that radiated into his 
chest and an EKG was obtained which was sinus rhythm without 
evidence of ischemia, with a few PVCs.  Electrolytes were 
normal.  Pain was noted to be sharp in character and unrelated 
to exertion.

Pulmonary: The patient remained stable from a pulmonary 
standpoint; vital signs were routinely monitored. Good pulmonary 
toilet, early ambulation and incentive spirometry were 
encouraged throughout hospitalization.

GI/GU/FEN: Post-operatively, the patient was made NPO with IV 
fluids. Diet was advanced to sips on POD1, and to full liquids 
on POD2, which was well tolerated.  Patient began to have stool 
and gas in ostomy and diet was advanced to POD2 to a regular 
diet.  Ostomy continued to put out gas and stool throughout this 
admission but was not putting out large volumes.  Patient was 
kept until POD 5 to continue ostomy teaching and to monitor 
ostomy output.  Patient's intake and output were closely 
monitored, and IV fluid was adjusted when necessary.  
Electrolytes were routinely followed, and repleted when 
necessary.  Patient retained a foley with his epidural, and 
foley was discontinued on POD2 after epidural was pulled and 
patient voided without incident.

ID: The patient's white blood count and fever curves were 
closely watched for signs of infection, of which there were 
none.  The incision remained clean, dry, and intact throughout 
this admission. 

Endocrine: The patient's blood sugar was monitored throughout 
his stay; insulin dosing was adjusted accordingly.

Hematology: The patient's complete blood count was examined 
routinely; no transfusions were required.

Prophylaxis: The patient received subcutaneous heparin and 
venodyne boots were used during this stay; was encouraged to get 
up and ambulate as early as possible.

At the time of discharge, the patient was doing well, afebrile 
with stable vital signs.  The patient was tolerating a regular 
diet, ambulating, voiding well, and pain was well controlled.  
The patient received discharge teaching and ___ 
instructions with understanding verbalized and agreement with 
the discharge plan.  Patient was set up with a visiting nurse 
and stated ___ continue to follow up with the ostomy team 
for his continued care.

MVI, probiotics
 
Discharge Medications:
1. acetaminophen 500 mg Tablet Sig: Two (2) Tablet PO Q6H (every 
6 hours).
Disp:*30 Tablet(s)* Refills:*2*
2. oxycodone 5 mg Tablet Sig: ___ Tablets PO every ___ hours as 
needed for pain.
Disp:*40 Tablet(s)* Refills:*0*

 
Discharge Disposition:
Home With Service
 
Facility:
___
 
Discharge Diagnosis:
Chronic ulcerative colitis, medically refractory.

 
Discharge Condition:
Mental Status: Clear and coherent.
Level of Consciousness: Alert and interactive.
Activity Status: Ambulatory - Independent.

 
Discharge Instructions:
___ were admitted to the inpatient general surgery unit. ___ 
have adequate pain control and have a tolerated a regular diet 
and may go home to continue your recovery.  

Please continue to monitor your ileostomy output. The most 
common complication from a new ileostomy placement is 
dehydration. The output from the stoma is stool from the small 
intestine and the water content is very high. The stool is no 
longer passing through the large intestine which is where the 
water from the stool is reabsorbed into the body and the stool 
becomes formed. ___ must measure your ileostomy output for the 
next few weeks. The output from the stoma should not be more 
than 1200 cc or less than 500 cc. If ___ find that your output 
has become too much or too little, please call the office for 
advice. The office nurse can recommend medications to increase 
or slow the ileostomy output. Keep yourself well hydrated, if 
___ notice your ileostomy output increasing, take in more 
electrolyte drink such as gatoraide. Please monitor yourself for 
signs and symptoms of dehydration including: dizziness 
(especially upon standing), weakness, dry mouth, headache, or 
fatigue. If ___ notice these symptoms please call the office or 
return to the emergency room for evaluation if these symptoms 
are severe. ___ may eat a modified regular diet with your new 
ileostomy.  

Please monitor the appearance of the ostomy and stoma and care 
for it as instructed by the wound/ostomy nurses. ___ stoma 
(intestine that protrudes outside of your abdomen) should be 
beefy red or pink, it may ooze small amounts of blood at times 
when touched and this should subside with time. The skin around 
the ostomy site should be kept clean and intact. Monitor the 
skin around the stoma for buldging or signs of infection listed 
above. Please care for the ostomy as ___ have been instructed by 
the wound/ostomy nurses. ___ will be able to make an appointment 
with the ostomy nurse in the clinic. A visiting nurse ___ who 
will help ___ monitor your ostomy until ___ are comfortable 
caring for it on your own. ___ have a long vertical incision on 
your abdomen that is closed with staples. This incision can be 
left open to air or covered with a dry sterile gauze dressing if 
the staples become irritated from clothing. The staples will 
stay in place until your first post-operative visit at which 
time they can be removed in the clinic, most likely by the 
office nurse. Please monitor the incision for signs and symptoms 
of infection including: increasing redness at the incision, 
opening of the incision, increased pain at the incision line, 
draining of white/green/yellow/foul smelling drainage, or if ___ 
develop a fever. 

Please call the office if ___ develop these symptoms or go to 
the emergency room if the symptoms are severe.  ___ may shower, 
let the warm water run over the incision line and pat the area 
dry with a towel, do not rub. 

No heavy lifting for at least 6 weeks after surgery unless 
instructed otherwise by your surgeon.  

___ will be prescribed a small amount of the pain medication. 
___ may take Tylenol as recommended for pain. Please do not take 
more than 4000 mg of Tylenol daily. Do not drink alcohol while 
taking narcotic pain medication or Tylenol. Please do not drive 
a car while taking narcotic pain medication. 

Thank ___ for allowing us to participate in your care. Our hope 
is that ___ will have a quick return to your life and usual 
activities. Best wishes.
 
Followup Instructions:
___
 

Output:
answer: The patient is a 31-year-old male who was admitted for a total abdominal colectomy with ileoanal pouch or end ileostomy due to chronic ulcerative colitis, which was medically refractory. The patient has a history of C. Diff colitis, ulcerative colitis, appendectomy, and right hand/finger debridement. He has no known allergies or adverse drug reactions. The patient underwent the procedure well and was discharged with good pain control. At discharge, the patient was tolerating a regular diet, ambulating, voiding well, and pain was well controlled. The patient was prescribed acetaminophen and oxycodone for pain management. He was also instructed to monitor his ileostomy output and to seek medical attention if he experiences dehydration symptoms.

Brief Hospital Course:
___ with h/o HTN, diabetes, and asthma presents with cough, sore

# Airway swelling: Patient given decadron, diphenhydramine and 
famotidine at OSH for concern of angioedema and upper airway 
swelling, transferred here for ENT and anesthesia evaluation 
should intubation be required. Receieved nebulized racemic

recently were most likely due to URI. CXR was without evidence 
of PNA. He was treated with saline nasal spray while in the 
MICU.

steroids and epinephrine. Appears to be sinus tachycardia on 
monitor. Troponin from the ED was negative.

wheezes on exam. He was given Albuterol nebulizers as needed.

# Diabetes: No active issues. He was discharged on his home dose 
of metformin.

blood pressure remained within the normal range. He was not 
restarted on any additional anti-hypertensive medications prior 
to discharge.

The patient's symptoms included a sensation of throat swelling, rhinorrhea, and a cough productive of whitish sputum. He was treated with steroids, benadryl, and famotidine, and his angioedema resolved. He was also diagnosed with asthma and treated with albuterol nebulizers as needed. The patient was discharged home with a diagnosis of angioedema and asthma, and was prescribed medications including diphenhydramine, famotidine, prednisone, albuterol inhaler, fluticasone propionate, and metformin.

answer:
The patient is a 70-year-old male who was admitted to the hospital with chest pain. He has a history of hypertension, dyslipidemia, pre-diabetes, hemorrhoids/anal fissure, and benign prostatic hypertrophy. He presented with substernal chest pain that started in the morning while he was lying in bed, which was associated with sweating, feeling hot and clammy, and diaphoresis. The pain lasted for about an hour and was described as severe. He denied radiation of pain, numbness/tingling in his arms, shortness of breath, and abdominal/back pain.

The patient has a past medical history of cardiac risk factors, including pre-diabetes, hypertension, and dyslipidemia. He also has a history of dermatoheliosis, benign prostatic hypertrophy, inflammatory colon polyp, chronic sun-damaged skin, and seborrheic keratoses.

The patient's physical examination on admission showed a well-developed male in no acute distress, with a normal cardiovascular examination. His laboratory results showed elevated troponin levels, with a peak level of 0.16 ng/mL. He was started on heparin gtt and aspirin.

The patient underwent a left heart catheterization, which showed 90% occlusion in the mid and distal left anterior descending (LAD) artery, and two drug-eluting stents were placed with good revascularization.

The patient's diagnosis is mild regional left ventricular systolic dysfunction, consistent with coronary artery disease. He was prescribed medications at discharge, including aspirin, clopidogrel, and metoprolol.

She has a history of primary sclerosing cholangitis (PSC), cirrhosis, ascites, hepatic encephalopathy, varices, and possibly Crohn's disease and celiac disease. She was diagnosed with cholangitis during a previous hospitalization and was treated with IV antibiotics. She has also had a laparoscopic cholecystectomy and liver biopsy, which showed bile ductular proliferation and early, incomplete nodule formation.

The patient's allergies include Bactrim, Ampicillin, Sulfa, cefuroxime, Gadolinium-Containing Contrast Media, gluten, nadolol, and Pneumovax 23. She has a history of hypertension, GERD, hypothyroidism, and OSA.

During her hospitalization, the patient's symptoms included abdominal pain, fever, nausea, and vomiting. She was treated with ertapenem and Zofran, and her nausea improved with the latter. Her laboratory results showed slightly elevated liver enzymes, but her chemistry panel and urinalysis were normal.

The patient underwent an ERCP procedure, which showed no dominant stricture in the CBD or CHD, but did show marked narrowing of the right and left common hepatic ducts consistent with known PSC. She also underwent an MRCP procedure, which showed cirrhosis complicated by portal hypertension, splenomegaly, and multiple esophageal and gastric varices.

At discharge, the patient was prescribed medications including ertapenem and Zofran, and was advised to follow up with her primary care physician.

Brief Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted to the general surgery service after 
presenting with complaint of epigastric and RUQ pain x several 
weeks. His initial labwork was notable for a leukocytosis of 
11.5, lipase of 129, TBR 1.1, and mild transaminitis. He 
underwent RUQ ultrasound which demonstrated gallbladder sludge 
but no evidence of cholelithiasis or cholecystitis. At this 
point, he was admitted to the medicine service due to concern 
for pancreatitis or potential choledocholithiasis given his lab 
abnormalites. He was started on bowel rest, IVF hydration, and 
IV ciprofloxacin/flagyl therapy. On HD2, he subsequently 
underwent MRCP which demonstrated mild pancreatitis, CBD 
dilation to 1.1cm, and gallbladder wall thickening concerning 
for acute cholecystitis.  He was transferred to the surgical 
service on HD2 in anticipation of cholecystectomy given these 
findings of cholecystitis in the absence of choledocholithiasis. 
At this point, his lipase had returned to normal and his 
abdominal pain had largely resolved. He was taken to the 
operating room on HD3 for laparoscopic cholecystectomy, which 
was uncomplicated and tolerated well. He did well 
post-operatively. His diet was gradually advanced from clears to 
regular, which he tolerated well. He voided appropriately and 
ambulated. His pain was well controlled on an oral regimen prior 
to discharge home. He is being discharged home on POD1 in stable 
condition to follow up in general surgery clinic in ___ weeks. 

Also of note, the patient complained of some pain in his right 
great toe, likely secondary to gout. He was continued on his 
home allopurinol and started on colchicine as needed, with 
improvement in his symptoms.

Discharge Condition:
Mental Status: Clear and coherent.
Level of Consciousness: Alert and interactive.
Activity Status: Ambulatory - Independent.

 
Discharge Instructions:
Dear Mr. ___, 
You were admitted to the general surgery service for treatment 
of your gallbladder infection. You were taken to the operating 
room for removal of your gallbladder, which you tolerated well. 
You did well post-operatively and are being discharged home in 
stable condition. You should follow up in general surgery clinic 
in one to two weeks. 
 
Please follow the post-operative directions below:

ACTIVITY:
- Do not drive until you have stopped taking pain medicine and 
feel you could respond in an emergency.
- You may climb stairs. You should continue to walk several 
times a day. 
- You may go outside, but avoid traveling long distances until 
you see your surgeon at your next visit. 
- You may start some light exercise when you feel comfortable. 
Slowly increase your activity back to your baseline as 
tolerated.
- Heavy exercise may be started after 6 weeks, but use common 
sense and go slowly at first. 
- No heavy lifting (10 pounds or more) until cleared by your 
surgeon, usually about 6 weeks. 

HOW YOU MAY FEEL: 
- You may feel weak or "washed out" for 6 weeks. You might want 
to nap often. Simple tasks may exhaust you.
- You may have a sore throat because of a tube that was in your 
throat during the surgery.

DIET:
You may have a regular diet as tolerated without restrictions. 

YOUR BOWELS:
-It is common to have some diarrhea after having your 
gallbladder removed. This should improve on it's own gradually. 
You can use immodium as needed if you have significant diarrhea 
until you are seen in clinic in ___ weeks. 
- Constipation is a common side effect of narcotic pain medicine 
such as oxycodone. If needed, you may take a stool softener 
(such as Colace) or gentle laxative (such as senna or miralax) 
twice a day. You can get any of these medicines without a 
prescription.
- If you go 48 hours without a bowel movement, or have pain 
moving the bowels, call your surgeon.

PAIN MANAGEMENT:
- You are being discharged with a prescription for oxycodone for 
pain control. You may take Tylenol as directed, not to exceed 
3500mg in 24 hours. Take regularly for a few days after surgery 
but you may skip a dose or increase time between doses if you 
are not having pain until you no longer need it. You may take 
the oxycodone for moderate and severe pain not controlled by the 
Tylenol. You may take a stool softener while on narcotics to 
help prevent the constipation that they may cause. Slowly wean 
off these medications as tolerated. 
- Your pain should get better day by day. If you find the pain 
is getting worse instead of better, please contact your surgeon.

If you experience any of the following, please contact your 
surgeon: 
- sharp pain or any severe pain that lasts several hours
- chest pain, pressure, squeezing, or tightness
- cough, shortness of breath, wheezing
- pain that is getting worse over time or pain with fever
- shaking chills, fever of more than 101
- a drastic change in nature or quality of your pain
- nausea and vomiting, inability to tolerate fluids, food, or 
your medications
- if you are getting dehydrated (dry mouth, rapid heart beat, 
feeling dizzy or faint especially while standing)
-any change in your symptoms or any symptoms that concern you

MEDICATIONS:
- Take all the medicines you were on before the operation just 
as you did before, unless you have been told differently.
- If you have any questions about what medicine to take or not 
to take, please call your surgeon. 

WOUND CARE: 
- dressing removal: Remove the outer dressing 48 hours after 
your surgery.
-You may shower starting tonight. Once you remove your outer 
dressing tomorrow, you can continue to shower with any bandage 
strips that may be covering your wound. Do not scrub and do not 
soak or swim, and pat the incision dry. If you have steri 
strips, they will fall off by themselves in ___ weeks. If any 
are still on in two weeks and the edges are curling up, you may 
carefully peel them off.  
-Do not take baths, soak, or swim for 6 weeks after surgery 
unless told otherwise by your surgical team. 
-Notify your surgeon is you notice abnormal (foul smelling, 
bloody, pus, etc) or increased drainage from your incision site, 
opening of your incision, or increased pain or bruising. Watch 
for signs of infection such as redness, streaking of your skin, 
swelling, increased pain, or increased drainage. 

Please call with any questions or concerns. Thank you for 
allowing us to participate in your care. We hope you have a 
quick return to your usual life and activities. 

-- Your ___ Care Team

 
                             ___ MD ___
 
Completed by: ___
 

Output:
answer: The patient is a 67-year-old male who was admitted to the hospital with a chief complaint of abdominal pain. He has a past medical history of depression, gout, and a suicide attempt. He was transferred from another hospital with worsening abdominal pain and concern for cholangitis. The patient underwent a laparoscopic cholecystectomy and was diagnosed with cholecystitis. He was started on IV fluids, antibiotics, and bowel rest, and his diet was gradually advanced to regular. He was discharged home on post-operative day 1 in stable condition, with instructions to follow up in the general surgery clinic in several weeks. The patient was prescribed medications for pain, constipation, and gout, and was advised to take acetaminophen as needed for pain.

Brief Hospital Course:
Patient Summary for Admission:
===============================
Ms ___ is a ___ yo woman with no major PMH presenting with RT 
leg pain over the past month with significant leukocytosis 
confirmed as CML. 

#CML
BCR-ABL confirmed on FISH analysis. Will initiate treatment 
after marrow for staging. Attempted bone marrow and only 
successful in getting Spleen size 14.5 and Eutos score 67.7 
predicts low risk disease. Initial testing was sent, G6PD 
remains pending, but infectious work up with HIV, hepatitis have 
been negative. TLS labs were followed. Patient was treated with 
hydroxyurea 1500 BID, fluids, and allopurinol. She was started 
on dastatinib. At time of discharge, Hydrea was stopped. Patient 
will continue ___nd continue Dasatinib.

#RT leg pain:
Fracture and clot have been excluded by XRAY and US. Other 
options include muscle strain, IT band strain, or bony pain from 
CML. MRI consistent with marrow CML infiltration. CML very 
rarely can create bone tumors that was initially a concern. No 
evidence of lytic lesions with no destruction of the cortex. 
Ortho was consulted and agreed she could resume weight bearing 
and this was most consistent with her normal CML. Pain initially 
treated with opioids, but patient had significant pain. She used 
gabapentin PRN as needed. Lidocaine patches were effective. 
Prior to discharge, patient transitioned to Tylenol for pain 
control.

#Dry cough
PNA vs pain associated. Possibly additional ___ pain. No 
findings on CXR. Cough resolved prior to discharge.

#Abdominal pain/nausea
Likely related to opioid use and anxiety combination. Opioids 
can last in system for 72 hours, will avoid for now. She also 
stopped her omeprazole during this admission due to TKI 
inhibitor interaction to acid reducers. Stopping antacids can 
cause increase in acid secretion which may have contributed to 
initial symptoms. Patient should not be discharged on antiacids. 
Nausea was controlled with Zofran and low dose Ativan. 

#Social complexity
#Anxiety
Complicated home situation and not surprising patient has 
significant anxiety. Patient amenable to following with a 
psychiatrist as an outpatient as she would like help coping and 
also working on her mood that she reports is labile. She will be 
referred to psych as an outpatient to someone near her on the 
___. 

#Contraception
Patient is obese, a women, and has a hematologic malignancy thus 
she should be treated without estrogens to prevent clot 
formation. She was started on norethindrone 3 weeks of pills 
followed by 1 week of no pills must take at the same time daily 
to prevent infection. She should use back up birth control with 
condoms for at least one month. OBGYN was used to help in 
decision making.

answer: The patient is a 22-year-old female who was admitted to the hospital with a chief complaint of right thigh pain and elevated WBC count. She has no major past medical history, but has had a laparoscopic cholecystectomy and has been diagnosed with GERD. She has allergies to penicillins and amoxicillin. The patient's physical examination revealed normal vital signs, with the exception of a slightly elevated blood pressure. Laboratory results showed an abnormal complete blood count, with a WBC count of 139.3 and a platelet count of 123. Bone marrow biopsy revealed a translocation involving chromosomes 9 and 22, consistent with chronic myelogenous leukemia. The patient was diagnosed with chronic myelogenous leukemia and underwent a bone marrow aspirate and biopsy. She was prescribed pain control medication and was discharged from the hospital.

He has a history of EtOH-induced chronic pancreatitis, SMV thrombosis, and is on Coumadin indefinitely. He also has chronic abdominal pain and is taking Oxycontin and Percocet. The patient's past medical history includes IDDM, chronic abdominal pain, chronic pancreatitis, SMV thrombosis, insomnia, and h/o alcoholism.

The patient's diagnosis includes a complex multiloculated cystic lesion in the right upper quadrant, possibly an abscess or hematoma, and a large loculated left pleural fluid collection. He also has sequela of chronic pancreatitis.

The patient underwent a tube thoracostomy (pigtail) and incision and drainage of the RUQ mass. He was prescribed medications at discharge, including antibiotics and pain medication.

The patient's physical exam revealed a thin male with a firm, extremely tender, erythematous mass deep to the RUQ scar. He had moderate TTP to palpation in the L hemi-abdomen, though he reported this was baseline. His vital signs were stable, and he was afebrile.

The patient's laboratory results showed an elevated white blood cell count, platelet count, and liver enzymes, consistent with an inflammatory process. His imaging studies, including CT and MRI, showed a complex multiloculated cystic lesion in the right upper quadrant, a large loculated left pleural fluid collection, and atrophy and calcification of the pancreas.

Overall, the patient's hospital course was marked by a complex and challenging presentation, requiring multiple procedures and a multidisciplinary approach to management.

changes, and always bring your card with you to any clinic or 
hospital visits.

You may shower. Allow the water to run over your incision and 
pat area dry. No rubbing, no lotions or powder near the 
incision. You may leave the incision open to the air. 
No tub baths or swimming
.
Drain and record the pigtail drain output twice daily and as 
needed so that the drain is never more than ½ full. Call the 
office if the drain output increases by more than 100 cc from 
the previous day,  becomes bloody or develops a foul odor.
Change the drain dressing once daily or after your shower. Do 
not allow the drain to hang freely at any time. Inspect the site 
for redness, drainage or bleeding. Make sure there is a stitch 
at the drain site.
.
No driving if taking narcotic pain medications and not until 
cleared by your surgeon.
.
Avoid direct sun exposure. Wear protective clothing and a hat, 
and always wear sunscreen with SPF 30 or higher when you go 
outdoors.
.

appetite will return with time. Eat small frequent meals, and

During his hospital stay, he developed atrial fibrillation, which was treated with digoxin and propranolol. He also developed a pericardial effusion, which was drained and treated with antibiotics. The patient's laboratory results showed normal liver function tests, and he was prescribed immunosuppressive medications, including Cellcept, myfortic, and tacrolimus. He was discharged with a drain in the mid abdominal collection and was advised to follow up with his healthcare provider for further treatment.

Discharge Instructions:
 
You were admitted to the hospital after you were hit by a line 
drive while playing baseball.  You sustained a fracture to the 
right side of your jaw.  You were taken to the operating room 
where you had your jaw repaired.  Your pain has been controlled 
with oral analgesia.  You were seen by the Oral surgeon prior to 
your discharge at which time many of your questions and concerns 
were addressed.  You are being discharged with the following 
instructions:  

 Wound care: Do not disturb or probe the surgical area with any 
objects. The sutures placed in your mouth are usually the type 
that self dissolve. If you have any sutures on the skin of your 
face or neck, your surgeon will remove them on the day of your 
first follow up appointment. SMOKING is detrimental to healing 
and will cause complications.

Bleeding: Intermittent bleeding or oozing overnight is normal. 
Placing fresh gauze over the area and biting on the gauze for 
___ minutes at a time may control the bleeding. If you had 
nasal surgery, you may have occasional slow oozing from your 
nostril for the first ___ days. Bleeding should never be severe. 
If bleeding persists or is severe or uncontrollable, please call 
our office immediately. If it is after normal business hours, 
please come to the emergency room and request that the oral 
surgery resident on call be paged.

___: Normal healing after oral surgery should be as follows: 
the first ___ days after surgery, are generally the most 
uncomfortable and there is usually significant swelling. After 
the first week, you should be more comfortable. The remainder of 
your postoperative course should be gradual, steady improvement. 
If you do not see continued improvement, please call our office.

Physical activity: It is recommended that you not perform any 
strenuous physical activity for a few weeks after surgery. Do 
not lift any heavy loads and avoid physical sports unless you 
obtain permission from your surgeon.

Swelling & Ice applications: Swelling is often associated with 
surgery. Swelling can be minimized by using a cold pack, ice bag 
or a bag of frozen peas wrapped in a towel, with firm 
application to face and neck areas. This should be applied 20 
minutes on and 20 minutes off during the first ___ days after 
surgery. If you have been given medicine to control the 
swelling, be sure to take it as directed.

Hot applications: Starting on the ___ or ___ day after surgery, 
you may apply warm compresses to the skin over the areas of 
swelling (hot water bottle wrapped in a towel, etc), for 20 
minutes on and 20 min off to help soothe tender areas and help 
to decrease swelling and stiffness. Please use caution when 
applying ice or heat to your face as certain areas may feel numb 
after surgery and extremes of temperature may cause serious 
damage.

Tooth brushing: Begin your normal oral hygiene the day after 
surgery. Soreness and swelling may nor permit vigorous brushing, 
but please make every effort to clean your teeth with the bounds 
of comfort. Any toothpaste is acceptable. Please remember that 
your gums may be numb after surgery. To avoid injury to the gums 
during brushing, use a child size toothbrush and brush in front 
of a mirror staying only on teeth.

Mouth rinses: Keeping your mouth clean after surgery is 
essential. Use 1 teaspoon of salt dissolved in an 8 ounce glass 
of warm water and gently rinse with portions of the solution, 
taking 5 min to use the entire glassful. Repeat as often as you 
like, but you should do this at least 4 times each day. If your 
surgeon has prescribed a specific rinse, use as directed.

Showering: You may shower ___ days after surgery, but please ask 
your surgeon about this. If you have any incisions on the skin 
of your face or body, you should cover them with a water 
resistant dressing while showering. DO NOT SOAK SURGICAL SITES. 
This will avoid getting the area excessively wet. As you may 
physically feel weak after surgery, initially avoid extreme hot 
or cold showers, as these may cause some patients to pass out. 
Also it is a good idea to make sure someone is available to 
assist you in case if you may need help.

___

The patient's surgical pain was controlled with intravenous analgesia and later converted to oral agents. He was started on clears and advanced to a full liquid diet. The patient was discharged home on post-operative day (POD) #1 in stable condition.

The patient's past medical history is unremarkable, and he has no known allergies or adverse drug reactions. His vital signs upon discharge were normal, with a temperature of 98.2°F, blood pressure of 143/62 mmHg, heart rate of 62 beats per minute, and respiratory rate of 18 breaths per minute.

The patient was prescribed three medications at discharge: chlorhexidine gluconate oral rinse, docusate sodium, and hydromorphone (Dilaudid) for pain control. He was instructed to follow a wound care regimen, including avoiding disturbing the surgical area and using gauze to control bleeding. He was also advised to avoid strenuous physical activity, use ice applications to minimize swelling, and begin normal oral hygiene the day after surgery.

The patient's medications at discharge include amlodipine, carvedilol, fluoxetine, rosuvastatin, lansoprazole, and lorazepam.

The patient's past medical history includes latent tuberculosis treated with INH, chronic hypertension, and chronic hemodialysis. His social history is significant for a sister who died from diabetes complications and multiple living children with various health problems.

At discharge, the patient's vital signs were stable, and he was prescribed medications for his conditions. His laboratory results showed normal blood chemistry values, except for slightly elevated glucose levels.

answer:
The patient is a 62-year-old female who presented with chest pain, dyspnea, and nausea. She was diagnosed with Non-ST Elevation Myocardial Infarction (NSTEMI) and underwent a percutaneous coronary intervention (PCI) procedure to treat a 90% stenosis in the proximal right coronary artery. The patient also had a history of hypertension, dyslipidemia, vitamin D deficiency, esophagus, kidney stones, medullary sponge disease, and rheumatoid arthritis. She had a family history of coronary artery disease (CAD) in her mother, father, and sister.

The patient's allergies include erythromycin base, iodine, latex, sulfa, and clindamycin. She was prescribed aspirin 81mg daily, Plavix 75mg daily, pantoprazole, losartan 50mg daily, and metoprolol succinate 25mg daily at discharge.

The patient's physical examination revealed a well-developed and well-nourished female with no significant skin lesions or rashes. Her vital signs were stable, and her lungs were clear bilaterally. The patient's electrocardiogram (EKG) showed normal sinus rhythm with no acute ST changes.

The patient's pertinent results included a coronary angiogram that showed ostial stenosis in the left main coronary artery and a 90% stenosis in the proximal right coronary artery. The PCI procedure was successful in treating the stenosis, and the patient was discharged with a plan for staged PCI in the future.

3. Sternal precautions
4. DO NOT lift anything heavier than 5 pounds or engage in 
strenuous activity for 6 weeks following surgery.
5. Please perform the occupational/physical therapy exercises as 
instructed.

Medications:
1. Resume your regular medications unless instructed otherwise 
and take any new meds as ordered .
2. No NSAIDs for one week. You may resume your baby aspirin 
seven days after discharge from the hospital.
3. You may take your prescribed pain medication for moderate to 
severe pain.  You may switch to Tylenol or Extra Strength 
Tylenol for mild pain as directed on the packaging.  Please note 
that Percocet and Vicodin have Tylenol as an active ingredient 
so do not take these meds with additional Tylenol.
4. Take prescription pain medications for pain not relieved by 
tylenol.
5. Take your antibiotic as prescribed.
6. Take Colace, 100 mg by mouth 2 times per day, while taking 
the prescription pain medication. You may use a different 
over-the-counter stool softerner if you wish.
7. Do not drive or operate heavy machinery while taking any 
narcotic pain medication. You may have constipation when taking 
narcotic pain medications (oxycodone, percocet, vicodin, 
hydrocodone, dilaudid, etc.); you should continue drinking 
fluids, you may take stool softeners, and should eat foods that 
are high in fiber.
.
Call the office IMMEDIATELY if you have any of the following:
1. Signs of infection: fever with chills, increased redness, 
welling, warmth or tenderness at the surgical site, or unusual 
drainage from the incision(s).
2. A large amount of bleeding from the incision(s) or drain(s).
3. Fever greater than 101.5 oF
4. Severe pain NOT relieved by your medication.
.
Return to the ER if: 
* If you are vomiting and cannot keep in fluids or your 
medications.

degrees or 38 (C) degrees, increased redness,swelling or 
discharge from incision, chest pain, shortness of breath, or 
anything else that is troubling you.
* Any serious change in your symptoms, or any new symptoms that 
concern you.
.

suction.

avoid taking pain medications on a daily basis unless prescribed

Discharge Instructions:
  
 
Dear Ms. ___,  
 
You were hospitalized due to symptoms of difficulty speaking 
resulting from an ACUTE ISCHEMIC STROKE, a condition where a 
blood vessel providing oxygen and nutrients to the brain is 
blocked by a clot. The brain is the part of your body that 
controls and directs all the other parts of your body, so damage 
to the brain from being deprived of its blood supply can result 
in a variety of symptoms.  
 
Stroke can have many different causes, so we assessed you for 
medical conditions that might raise your risk of having stroke. 
In order to prevent future strokes, we plan to modify those risk 
factors. Your risk factors are:  
- High blood pressure
- High cholesterol
 
We are changing your medications as follows:  
- Stop taking aspirin and start Plavix 75mg daily. 
 
Please take your other medications as prescribed.  
 
Please followup with Neurology and your primary care physician.  

 
If you experience any of the symptoms below, please seek 
emergency medical attention by calling Emergency Medical 
Services (dialing 911). In particular, since stroke can recur, 
please pay attention to the sudden onset and persistence of 
these symptoms:  
- sudden partial or complete loss of vision  
- sudden loss of the ability to speak words from your mouth  
- sudden loss of the ability to understand others speaking to 
you  
- sudden weakness of one side of the body  
- sudden drooping of one side of the face  
- sudden loss of sensation of one side of the body  
 
Sincerely,

The patient is a 75-year-old female who was admitted to the hospital with a chief complaint of persistent dysarthria. She has a history of end-stage renal disease (ESRD) on hemodialysis (HD), hypertension, diabetes, and a lifetime of smoking. She also has a history of long QT syndrome, which was complicated by a ventricular tachycardia (VT) arrest after receiving azithromycin. 

The patient's past medical history includes type 2 diabetes, rheumatoid arthritis, hypertension, hyperlipidemia, renal tubular acidosis, peripheral vascular disease, sciatica, cataract, and unspecified sensorineural hearing loss. 

On physical examination, the patient was awake, cooperative, and oriented to her name, location, and date. Her language was fluent, but she had mild dysarthria. Her cranial nerves were intact, and her motor strength was full throughout. 

The patient's laboratory results showed a hemoglobin A1c level of 7.9%, triglycerides of 107, and a thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH) level of 1.0. 

The patient's imaging studies showed approximately 25% stenosis of the left proximal internal carotid artery, severe stenosis of the origin of the left vertebral artery, and intracranial atherosclerotic disease. 

The patient was discharged with a diagnosis of mild dysarthria and recommendations for further evaluation and management.

The patient is a 45-year-old male who was admitted to the hospital with sepsis. He initially presented to the emergency department with perforated appendicitis, which was complicated by an abscess that was drained twice. He was treated with antibiotics and discharged, but two weeks later, he returned to the hospital with sepsis and was admitted to the intensive care unit (ICU). 

The patient has a history of MRSA abscess on his arm and a previous appendectomy complicated by an abscess. He has no known allergies to drugs. 

On admission, the patient's physical examination revealed an ill-appearing individual with a temperature of 98.4°F, blood pressure of 129/67 mmHg, and a heart rate of 88 beats per minute. His abdomen was soft, nontender, and nondistended, with well-healed laparoscopic scars. 

Laboratory results showed a white blood cell count of 24.7, lactate level of 1.8, and troponin level of 0.29. He was treated with antibiotics, including vancomycin, levofloxacin, and ceftriaxone, and was started on a norepinephrine drip. 

The patient's diagnosis was sepsis, and he was managed non-surgically. He was discharged with a plan for continued antibiotic therapy and follow-up.

Overall, the patient's condition appears to be stable, and she was discharged with a plan for continued treatment and follow-up.

Activity Status: Ambulatory with assistance.
 
Discharge Instructions:
Surgery:
- You underwent surgery to remove a brain mass from your brain.
- You may shower at this time, but please keep your surgical 
incision dry until your sutures are removed. 
- It is best to keep your surgical incision open to air, but it 
is okay to cover it while outside. 
- Please call your neurosurgeon if there are any signs of 
infection, such as fever, redness, swelling, or drainage from 
the surgical incision.

Activity:
- You may take leisurely walks and slowly increase your activity 
at your own pace. ___ try to do too much all at once.
- We recommend that you avoid heavy lifting, running, climbing, 
or other strenuous exercise until your follow-up.
- No driving while taking narcotics or any other sedating 
medications.
- If you have experienced a seizure, you are not allowed to 
drive by law.
- No contact sports until cleared by your neurosurgeon.

Medications:
- You may use acetaminophen (Tylenol) for minor discomfort if 
you are not otherwise restricted from taking this medication.
- Please do not take any blood thinning medications, such as 
aspirin, clopidogrel (Plavix), ibuprofen, warfarin (Coumadin), 
etc. until cleared by your neurosurgeon.
- You have been discharged on levetiracetam (Keppra). This 
medication helps to prevent seizures. Please continue this 
medication as indicated. It is important that you take this 
medication consistently and on time.

What You ___ Experience:
- You may experience pain at the surgical incision and 
headaches.
- Feeling more tired or restless is common.
- You may also experience some postoperative swelling around 
your face and eyes. This is normal after surgery and most 
noticeable on the ___ and ___ days after surgery. You may apply 
ice or a cool or warm washcloth to help with this. It will be 
its worst in the morning after laying flat from sleeping, but 
should decrease once up.
- You may experience soreness with chewing. This is normal from 
the surgery and will improve with time. Softer foods may be 
easier during this time. 
- Constipation is common. Be sure to drink plenty of fluids and 
eat a high fiber diet. You may also try and over-the-counter 
stool softener if needed.

Please Call Your Neurosurgeon At ___ If You Experience:
- Severe pain, redness, swelling, or drainage from the surgical 
incision.
- Fever greater than 101.5 degrees Fahrenheit.
- Severe headaches not relieved with prescribed pain 
medications.
- Extreme sleepiness and not being able to stay awake.
- Nausea or vomiting.
- Seizures.
- Weakness or changes in sensation in your face, arms, or legs.
- Any new problems with your vision or ability to speak.

Call ___ And Go To The Nearest Emergency Department If You 
Experience Any Of The Following: 
- Sudden severe headaches with no known reason.
- Sudden confusion or trouble speaking or understanding.
- Sudden weakness or numbness in the face, arms, or legs.
- Sudden dizziness, trouble walking, or loss of balance or 
coordination.
 
Followup Instructions:
___

answer: The patient is a 56-year-old female who was admitted to the hospital with a chief complaint of clumsy left hand, falls. She has a history of ovarian mass resected, asthma, and EtOH cirrhosis. On admission, her physical exam revealed subtle anisocoria, left facial droop, and left drift. She was diagnosed with a right temporoparietal brain mass and underwent a right craniotomy for resection. During her hospital stay, she developed hypertension, elevated INR, and low platelets, which were managed with medications. She also experienced anxiety and paranoia, which were treated with steroids and Xanax. At the time of discharge, she was tolerating a regular diet, ambulating without difficulty, and had stable vital signs. Her medications at discharge included acetaminophen, albuterol, amlodipine, calcium carbonate, dexamethasone, and others. She was discharged to an extended care facility.

Brief Hospital Course:
Ms. ___ is a ___ with history of HTN, COPD, IDDM, 
hypothyroidism, DVT who originally presented with diarrhea, SOB, 
and headache x ___ days, found to have severe hypervolemic 
hyponatremia that improved with diuresis and discontinuation of 
her hydrochlorathiazide. She was found to have iron deficiency 
anemia and dysphagia for which she underwent EGD and colonoscopy 
without pertinent findings.

ACTIVE ISSUES
=======================
#Hypotonic, Hypervolemic Hyponatremia
Admitted with severe hyponatremia to 111. Etiology was mostly 
hypervolemia due to diastolic heart failure exacerbation and 
HCTZ use. We d/c'd HCTZ and placed a fluid restriction and 
initiated pharmacologic diuresis with loop diuretics until the 
patient was euvolemic. Patient originally had symptomatic 
headaches, confusion, and shortness of breath; these all 
improved with diuresis. Renal was consulted and made 
recommendations about an outpatient diuretic regimen with 
torsemide 10 mg PO QD. The patient's Na normalized to 141 by 
discharge and she was asymptomatic. 

#Heart failure with preserved ejection fraction
Patient originally presented with severe bilateral lower 
extremity edema, orthopnea, shortness of breath, and severely 
elevated BNP. LENIs were negative. Responded well to diuresis as 
above. Was euvolemic at discharge. Discharge weight: 110.4 kg. 
Discharge Cr: 0.8.

#Anemia
Hgb remained consistently low with microcytic pattern during 
admission. Ferritin was low-normal and TIBC was high-normal. 
Patient also described ongoing weight loss and change in stool 
patterns (alternating diarrhea/constipation + worm-like stools). 
Last colonoscopy in ___ included removal of 17 polyps and 
recommendation for follow-up colonoscopy in ___ year, which 
patient did not get. She received both a colonoscopy and an EGD 
as an inpatient. Had one colonic polyp removed and random 
biopsies sent. EGD was notable for mild gastritis with antral 
erosions (no stricture). 

#Change in stool habits
Patient reported 10 days of watery diarrhea prior to admission. 
She also described change in stool formation 
("worm/pebble-like"). Diarrhea was likely viral gastroenteritis 
given time course. C diff was negative. After admission patient 
was constipated for 1 week. This resolved with a bowel prep that 
was done in preparation of an inpatient colonoscopy to evaluate 
for iron deficiency anemia (see above). IgA levels and 
transglutaminase antibodies were sent, both negative.  

#Dysphagia
Patient complained of discomfort while swallowing during 
admission. Was evaluated by speech and swallow who found no 
oropharyngeal pathology. EGD showed mild gastritis, no evidence 
of esophageal stricture. 

#Klebsiella UTI
Patient had UA concerning for infection upon admission, 
speciated to Klebsiella. Was treated with ceftriaxone x 3 days 
with good result. Subsequently denied urinary discomfort. 

#Vulvovaginal candidiasis
#Urinary retention
Patient had vaginal discharge and inner groin rash consistent 
with candidiasis. Responded very well to PO fluconazole and 
miconazole powder. Pt originally had Foley upon admission which 
was discontinued. Pt had one day of urinary retention which 
later resolved. Likely was due to UTI / prolonged Foley 
placement. 

# Hypoxia/dyspnea
Patient had acute on chronic dyspnea during hospitalization. Has 
40 pack year smoking history and COPD. Generally felt with 
activity. CXR without evidence of pulmonary edema, pneumonia, or 
pleural effusion. LENIs negative as above. Patient's oxygenation 
improved with 2L NC, later weaned to RA. She was also given 
standing Duonebs. 

#Hyperglycemia
Patient was managed on an insulin sliding scale. PO 
anti-hyperglycemics were held.

#Sore throat
Patient complained of sore throat that was managed with throat 
lozenges and chloraseptic spray with good response. Likely a 
viral pharyngitis. No erythema or exudates on exam. 

#Hypomagensia
Patient had hypomagnesmia upon admission that normalized with 
administration of MgSO4.

CHRONIC ISSUES
========================
# History of seizures: Continued home keppra

mg but holding off currently i/s/o ongoing diuresis; continue

any new medications as prescribed.

The patient underwent an open reduction and internal fixation (ORIF) of the right tibia fracture and was discharged with a diagnosis of a displaced segmental fracture of the shaft of the right tibia. He was prescribed medications including bisacodyl, docusate sodium, enoxaparin sodium, oxyCODONE, senna, aspirin, and metFORMIN. 

At the time of discharge, the patient's pain was well-controlled, and his incisions were clean and dry. He was advised to follow up with his surgeon and to take his medications as prescribed. He was also instructed to avoid drinking alcohol, driving, or operating machinery while taking narcotic pain relievers and to take a stool softener to prevent constipation.

The patient is a 61-year-old male who was admitted to the hospital with a chief complaint of s/p DDLT, elevated LFTs, and hyponatremia. He has a history of HCV cirrhosis and HCC, and has undergone a deceased donor liver transplant with Roux-en-Y hepaticojejunostomy. He has also had complications such as biliary stricture, hepatic artery thrombosis, sepsis, VRE bacteremia, malnutrition, biloma, and multiple intraabdominal collections.

The patient's allergies include NSAIDs. He has a history of insulin-dependent diabetes, HCV cirrhosis, HCC, and other medical conditions.

The patient's diagnosis is not explicitly stated in the report, but it appears that he has a biliary stricture and hyponatremia.

The patient underwent a cholangiogram and manipulation of his drain, and was found to have occlusion of the distal portion of his PTBD. He was exchanged for a new internal-external PTBD, which was attached to a bag for external drainage.

The patient's medications at discharge include acetaminophen, aspirin, benzonatate, cefepime, clopidogrel, daptomycin, docusate sodium, enoxaparin sodium, fluconazole, gabapentin, hydrocodone, insulin, olanzapine, pantoprazole, polyethylene glycol, prednisone, psyllium powder, sulfameth/trimethoprim, and tacrolimus.

The patient's physical exam on admission showed him to be well appearing, with no scleral icterus, midline trachea, and neck supple. His vital signs were normal, and his abdomen was soft, nontender, and nondistended. His drains were producing scant turbid bilious drainage.

The patient's labs on admission showed elevated LFTs and hyponatremia, but his labs at discharge showed improvement in these values. His blood culture was negative.

The patient's plan for continued drain management is to exchange his PTBD and abscess drain in 4 weeks. He will continue to receive IV antibiotics and will be discharged home on the same regimen of daptomycin and cefepime via his existing PICC line.

Discharge Instructions
Spine Surgery without Fusion

Surgery
 Your dressing was removed the second day after surgery. 
 Your incision is closed with staples. You will need staple 
removal. Please keep your incision dry until staple removal.
 Do not apply any lotions or creams to the site. 
 Please avoid swimming for two weeks after suture/staple 
removal.
 Call your surgeon if there are any signs of infection like 
redness, fever, or drainage. 

Activity
 We recommend that you avoid heavy lifting, running, climbing, 
or other strenuous exercise until your follow-up appointment.
 You make take leisurely walks and slowly increase your 
activity at your own pace. ___ try to do too much all at once.
 No driving while taking any narcotic or sedating medication. 
 No contact sports until cleared by your neurosurgeon. 

Medications
 ***Please do NOT take any blood thinning medication (Aspirin, 
Ibuprofen, Plavix, Coumadin) until cleared by the neurosurgeon. 
 You may use Acetaminophen (Tylenol) for minor discomfort if 
you are not otherwise restricted from taking this medication.
 It is important to increase fluid intake while taking pain 
medications. We also recommend a stool softener like Colace. 
Pain medications can cause constipation. 

When to Call Your Doctor at ___ for:
 Severe pain, swelling, redness or drainage from the incision 
site. 
 Fever greater than 101.5 degrees Fahrenheit
 New weakness or changes in sensation in your arms or legs.

___
 

Output:
answer:
Mrs. [Name], a 60-year-old female, was admitted to the hospital for lumbar stenosis. She had undergone an L3-L4 laminectomy for lumbar stenosis in the past and had developed pain down her right leg over the last year. She underwent an elective L2-3 and L4-5 laminectomies plus right L4-5 microdiscectomy, which was uneventful initially. However, she developed right lower extremity weakness and new radicular pain, and a small hematoma collection was found, which required re-exploration. She was discharged with paresthesias in her right lower extremity and weakness in her right hip flexor and knee flexors and extensors.

The patient has a history of diabetes, cutaneous candidiasis, hip pain, vitamin D deficiency, hepatic steatosis, hypothyroidism, sleep apnea, and hypertension. She has allergies to penicillins, cephalosporins, codeine, Lipitor, econazole, nitrate, peppers, Flagyl, ProAir HFA, sage, nystatin, and Victoza.

The patient's diagnosis is lumbar stenosis, and she underwent procedures including L2-3 and L4-5 laminectomies and right L4-5 microdiscectomy. She was prescribed medications including acetaminophen, bisacodyl, diazepam, docusate sodium, gabapentin, glucagon, heparin, insulin, milk of magnesia, oxyCODONE, polyethylene glycol, senna, artificial tears, atenolol, levothyroxine, pravastatin, and vitamin D.

The patient was discharged to extended care and was instructed to follow up with her neurosurgeon. She was advised to avoid heavy lifting, running, climbing, or other strenuous exercise until her follow-up appointment and to take pain medications as directed.

The patient is an 85-year-old male who was admitted with shortness of breath and cough for several weeks. He has a history of hypertension, diet-controlled type II diabetes mellitus, and renal carcinoma, which was surgically treated with a partial right nephrectomy. He also has a history of benign prostatic hypertrophy, testicular microlithiasis, left eye blindness, and supraventricular tachycardia. The patient has allergies to morphine and has been experiencing worsening symptoms over the past month.

Upon admission, the patient's vital signs were stable, and he was noted to be in atrial fibrillation with a rapid ventricular response. He was treated with diltiazem and levoquin, and his symptoms improved. The patient's physical examination revealed coarse breath sounds with crackles at bilateral bases, and his chest X-ray showed severe cardiomegaly with bilateral pleural effusions.

The patient's laboratory results showed elevated troponin levels, indicating cardiac damage, and his electrocardiogram showed atrial fibrillation with left axis deviation and left anterior fascicular block. His echocardiogram revealed mild symmetric left ventricular hypertrophy and moderate regional left ventricular systolic dysfunction.

At discharge, the patient was prescribed medications including diltiazem, levoquin, and ASA, and he was advised to continue his medications as directed.

Dr. ___

The patient's medications at discharge include acetaminophen, citalopram, docusate sodium, famotidine, lisinopril, memantine, oxycodone, and dexamethasone. He was also prescribed physical therapy and instructed to follow up with his surgeon.

The patient's discharge diagnosis includes metastatic carcinoma, posterior fossa brain mass, and cerebral edema. His discharge condition is stable, with a clear and coherent mental status, alert and interactive level of consciousness, and ambulatory independence.

answer:
The patient is a 62-year-old male with a chief complaint of disseminated fusarium, VRE bacteremia. He has a past medical history of biphenotypic leukemia, which was initially treated with steroids and IVIG, and later with hyperCVAD and 7+3 chemotherapy. He was also diagnosed with disseminated fusarium infection and treated with Ambisome and voriconazole. The patient has no known allergies or adverse drug reactions.

The patient was admitted to the hospital for a new lesion on his congenital oral mass, which is concerning for persistent and worsening fungal infection. He has been experiencing pain in his mouth, but has not taken pain medication. He also developed painless, nonpruritic erythematous blanching patches on his shins bilaterally.

The patient's physical examination revealed a pleasant elderly male in no acute distress, with a normal chest and abdominal examination. He had petechiae on his lower abdomen, legs, and feet, as well as erythematous, blanching patches on his anterior shins with superficial ulcerations bilaterally.

The patient's laboratory results showed a white blood cell count of 0.3, with 28% blasts, and a platelet count of 10%. His liver function tests were abnormal, with elevated levels of ALT, AST, and LDH. His blood cultures were positive for VRE.

The patient underwent a bone marrow biopsy and skin biopsy, and was treated with decitabine and Ambisome. He was also prescribed medications at discharge, including voriconazole and Lamivudine.

Overall, the patient's diagnosis is disseminated fusarium infection, VRE bacteremia, and biphenotypic leukemia. He is being treated with antifungal and antibacterial medications, and will require ongoing monitoring and management of his condition.

The patient's past medical history includes asthma, obesity, vitamin D deficiency, sickle cell trait, depression, and chronic back pain. She has also had several hospitalizations for abdominal pain, nausea, and vomiting, as well as cholangitis, and has undergone ERCP and stent placement.

The patient's diagnosis was confirmed by a liver biopsy, and she underwent a left hepatic trisegmentectomy with roux-en-Y hepaticojejunostomy, as well as other procedures including washout, oversewing bowel, PV thrombectomy, open abdomen, and tracheostomy. At discharge, the patient was prescribed medications including antibiotics and pain medication.

The patient is a 77-year-old male who was admitted to the hospital due to mental status changes, failure to thrive, and a history of agitation and aggression. He has a past medical history of diabetes, coronary artery disease, hypertension, asthma, and chronic kidney disease. He was previously admitted to a skilled nursing facility (SNF) for similar symptoms and was discharged with a diagnosis of mild cognitive delay and confusion over his disposition. He was readmitted to the hospital after being transferred from the SNF, where he was found to have an excoriated red rash and a stage I coccygeal ulcer. He was given miconazole powder and haldol 1 mg IV in the emergency department.

The patient's physical examination revealed an elderly obese male who was alert and hypervigilant, but clearly delirious. His mental status was impaired, and he was unable to give a coherent history. His neurological examination was normal, except for increased tone and jerks in his left upper extremity.

The patient's laboratory results showed a normal complete blood count, electrolyte panel, and renal function tests. His urine analysis showed a specific gravity of 1.020, and his blood glucose level was 159 mg/dL. His electroencephalogram (EEG) was mildly abnormal, showing occasional bursts of generalized theta slowing.

The patient underwent a lumbar puncture, which showed a clear and colorless cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) with no white blood cells, red blood cells, or protein. His magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of the head and neck showed stable mild chronic small vessel ischemic changes and moderate diffuse parenchymal volume loss.

The patient was diagnosed with dementia, likely due to his rapid progression of cognitive decline. He was treated with haldol and reassurance, and his agitation and aggression improved. He was discharged from the hospital with a plan for further management and follow-up.

Dear Ms. ___,

You have undergone a laparoscopic sleeve gastrectomy, recovered 
in the hospital and are preparing for discharge to home with the 
following instructions:

Please call your surgeon or return to the Emergency Department 
if you develop a fever greater than ___ F, shaking chills, chest 
pain, difficulty breathing, pain with breathing, cough, a rapid 
heartbeat, dizziness, severe abdominal pain, pain unrelieved by 
your pain medication, a change in the nature or severity of your 
pain, severe nausea, vomiting, abdominal bloating, severe 
diarrhea, inability to eat or drink, foul smelling or colorful

incisions, or any other symptoms which are concerning to you.
Diet: Stay on Stage III diet until your follow up appointment; 
please refer to your work book for detailed instructions. Do not 
self- advance your diet and avoid drinking with a straw or 
chewing gum. To avoid dehydration, remember to sip small amounts 
of fluid frequently throughout the day to reach a goal of 
approximately ___ mL per day. Please note the following signs 
of dehydration: dry mouth, rapid heartbeat, feeling dizzy or 
faint, dark colored urine, infrequent urination.

Please refer to the medication list provided with your discharge 
paperwork for detailed instruction regarding your home and newly 
prescribed medications.

1.  Pain medication: You will receive a prescription for liquid 
oxycodone, an opioid pain medication.  This medication will make 
you drowsy and impair your ability to drive a motor vehicle or 
operate machinery safely. You MUST refrain from such activities 
while taking these medications. You may also take acetaminophen 
(Tylenol) for pain management; do not exceed 4000 mg per 24 hour 
period.
2. Constipation: This is a common side effect of opioid pain 
medication.  If you experience constipation, please reduce or 
eliminate opioid pain medication.  You may trial 2 ounces of 
light prune juice and/or a stool softener (i.e. crushed docusate 
sodium tablets), twice daily until you resume a normal bowel 
pattern.  Please stop taking this medication if you develop 
loose stools. Please do not begin taking laxatives including 
until you have discussed it with your nurse or surgeon.
3. Antacids: You will be taking famotidine tablets, 20 mg twice 
daily, for one month. This medicine reduces stomach acid 
production. Please crush.
4. You must not use NSAIDS (non-steroidal anti-inflammatory 
drugs). Examples include, but are not limited to Aleve, 
Arthrotec, aspirin, Bufferin, diclofenac, Ecotrin, etodolac, 
ibuprofen, Indocin, indomethacin, Feldene, ketorolac, 
meclofenamate, meloxicam, Midol, Motrin, nambumetone, Naprosyn, 
Naproxen, Nuprin, oxaprozin, Piroxicam, Relafen, Toradol and 
Voltaren. These agents may cause bleeding and ulcers in your 
digestive system. If you are unclear whether a medication is 
considered an NSAID, please ask call your nurse or ask your 
pharmacist.
5.  Vitamins/ minerals: You may resume a chewable multivitamin, 
however, please discuss when to resume additional vitamin and 
mineral supplements with your bariatric dietitian.
6.  Please hold chlorthalidone at this time and notify your 
primary care provider and prescribing provider of this 
medication change immediately.  Please monitor your blood 
pressure daily; notify your PCP if you have elevated or low 
readings.  Also, please see immediate medical attention should 
you experience a severe headache, difficulty walking/ talking, 
weakness, lightheadedness or any other symptoms that are 
concerning to you.

Activity:
You should continue walking frequently throughout the day right 
after surgery; you may climb stairs.  
You may resume moderate exercise at your discretion, but avoid 
performing abdominal exercises or lifting items greater than10 
to 15 pounds for six weeks.

Wound Care:
You may remove any remaining gauze from over your incisions.  
You will have thin paper strips (Steri-Strips) over your 
incision; please, remove any remaining Steri-Strip seven to 10 
days after surgery.   
You may shower 48 hours following your surgery; avoid scrubbing 
your incisions and gently pat them dry.  Avoid tub baths or 
swimming until cleared by your surgeon. 
If there is clear drainage from your incisions, cover with 
clean, dry gauze. 
Please call the doctor if you have increased pain, swelling, 
redness, cloudy, bloody or foul smelling drainage from the 
incision sites. 
Avoid direct sun exposure to the incision area for up to 24 
months.
Do not use any ointments on the incision unless you were told 
otherwise.

___ y/o M with HCV cirrhosis c/b ascites, refractory to 
increasing medical diuresis, requiring admission for 
paracentesis, in setting of increased dyspnea. Found to have 
pulmonary emboli.

# HCV Cirrhosis/Abdominal distension: The patient initially 
underwent an attempted bedside paracentesis, which only yielded 
small amounts of hemorrhagic ascites. He then underwent 
ultrasound-guided diagnostic/therapeutic paracenteses x 2, each 
of which was negative for infection or malignancy. He received 
IV albumin with the procedures. He reported somewhat improved 
respiratory status after the paracenteses, often alluding to his 
new ability to finish a sentence without having to take a 
breath. MRI showed stable solitary liver lesion. LFTs were 
generally stable. His furosemide and spironolactone were 
initally held, then restarted. He was discharged on his home 
dose of spironolactone, which has caused him side effects 
(breast tenderness) on higher doses. He was also discharged on 
an increased dose of furosemide, with instructions to have his 
electrolytes and renal function checked at his primary care 
follow-up visit on ___. Of note, the patient's paracentesis 
site on his RLQ continued to leak ascites for several days. 
Ostomy bag was placed to collect the ascites, and the patient 
ultimately had multiple stitches placed over the paracentesis 
site, to prevent further leakage. The stitch was non-absorbable, 
and will have to be removed at a follow-up visit.

# Pulmonary Emboli: The patient was persistently tachycardic and 
remained short of breath after his large-volume paracentesis. 
ABG revealed hypoxia, and the patient desaturated to the 
mid-high 80's when ambulatory. Lower extremity ultrasound was 
negative for deep venous thrombosis. He had CTA performed, which 
revealed bilateral segmental/sub-segmental pulmonary emboli, and 
was immediately initiated on a heparin drip, without a bolus (no 
bolus ___ concern over hemorrhagic ascites). He was initiated on 
warfarin as well, and took several nights of ___ mg warfarin, 
to reach a therapeutic INR of 2.7, on day of discharge. He was 
discharged on warfarin 5 mg PO QHS. Discussions took place 
between the patient's primary care physician and the admitting 
team, and the patient was scheduled to see the PCP on ___ 
___, where the PCP would assume responsibility for checking 
INR's and managing the patient's warfarin regimen.

No clear cause for PE was identified, as the patient reported up 
to date cancer screening (no malignancy on colonoscopy/biopsy, 
normal PSA), and did not have any clear risk factors for blood 
clots.

# Hypertension: The patient's blood pressure was consistently 
normotensive during the admission. He was discharged on 
furosemide and spironolactone, as mentioned above.
.
# Depression/Anxiety: The patient demonstrated a good mood with 
little anxiety, during the admission. He was continued on his 
home regimen of amitriptyline.

You were admitted to the hospital to undergo a paracentesis for 
your increasing abdominal distension. Bedside paracentesis 
yielded small amounts of bloody fluid, and the attempt was 
aborted. The following day you underwent paracentesis with 
radiologic guidance, which yielded 5 liters of fluid, which was 
sent for analysis. You had a repeat paracentesis done several 
days later. Each time, your abdominal fluid was negative for

.
You had low oxygen levels when walking, so you had a CT scan of 
your chest, which showed pulmonary emboli (blood clots) in both

through the IV and later in pill form. Your coumadin levels were 
at the appropriate levels on the day of discharge, but you will

should also have your electrolytes and kidney function tests 
checked by your PCP, as we have changed your diuretic

.

.

He has been experiencing increasing abdominal distension, lower extremity edema, and dyspnea, which has worsened over time. He has a history of hypertension, depression, anxiety, migraines, and cellulitis. He is also obese and has a family history of alcoholism.

The patient's physical examination revealed an obese male with a distended abdomen, bilateral 2+ pitting edema, and ecchymosis over the proximal right thigh. His laboratory results showed abnormal liver function tests, including elevated ALT and AST levels, as well as abnormal coagulation studies. His renal function was also impaired, with elevated BUN and creatinine levels.

The patient underwent diagnostic and therapeutic paracentesis, and his medications at discharge included furosemide, spironolactone, and lactulose. He was also prescribed bupropion, which he had recently started taking.
