answer:
The patient is a 56-year-old male who presented with ankle swelling and drainage. He has a history of diabetes, hypertension, hyperlipidemia, colon cancer, cirrhosis, and atrial fibrillation. He was previously admitted to another hospital for the same condition and was discharged home with plans to follow up in clinic. However, he developed increased swelling and low-grade fever, and was referred to the current hospital for admission.

The patient's allergies include erythromycin base. He has a history of MRSA infection and was previously treated with vancomycin and doxycycline. He was prescribed lasix to increase his dosage due to swelling, and also took a leftover prescription of dicloxacillin at home.

The patient's diagnosis is mixed bacterial flora, including Staphylococcus aureus, which is resistant to oxacillin, erythromycin, and other antibiotics. He underwent bedside bone biopsy, which showed fibro-purulent exudate with associated fragments of necrotic bone.

The patient underwent imaging studies, including X-ray and CT scans, which showed chronic changes in the ankle without evidence of osteomyelitis. However, the CT scan did show findings worrisome for calcaneal ulcer with associated phlegmon.

The patient's medications at discharge include lasix and other antibiotics.

The patient's bleeding resolved with the application of Surgicel and direct pressure, but she experienced recurrent bleeding when she got up to use the bathroom. A CT scan revealed a fluid collection with surrounding stranding, consistent with an abscess. The patient was treated with antibiotics and was advised to remain non-weightbearing. She was also prescribed medications including acetaminophen, furosemide, levothyroxine, and valacyclovir.

The patient's past medical history is significant for hypertension, hypothyroidism, COPD, and prior DVT. She also has a history of breast cancer, asthma, and ocular herpes. Her social history is significant for a motor vehicle accident in which she believes part of her skull was removed.

The patient's physical examination revealed a firm hematoma in her left upper thigh, as well as a chronic appearing wound with no active bleeding. Her vital signs were stable, and her laboratory results showed a decrease in her hemoglobin level. The patient was advised to remain non-weightbearing and to follow up with her primary care physician for further evaluation and treatment.

The patient is a 54-year-old female who was admitted to the hospital with a chief complaint of seizures. She has a history of chronic myeloid leukemia (CML) that converted to acute myeloid leukemia (AML) after a cord blood transplant. She has also had a history of breast cancer and has undergone several surgeries, including sinus surgery. The patient has allergies to cefepime and ragweed. She was started on immunosuppressive therapy and has been experiencing recurrent pulmonary exacerbations.

The patient's past medical history includes GVHD, BOOP, chronic sinusitis, chronic renal failure, breast cancer, glaucoma, depression, and oophorectomy. She has also had a history of BK viremia and HHV-6 viremia.

The patient's physical examination revealed that she was alert and well-appearing, with no acute distress. Her vital signs were normal, and her neurological examination was normal except for an unsteady gait and need for support while walking.

The patient's laboratory results showed elevated white blood cell count, hyponatremia, and mild acute renal failure. She was started on vancomycin, levaquin, and posaconazole, and was observed off of antibiotics for 24 hours without any signs of infection.

The patient's diagnosis was acute lymphoblastic crisis of CML, and she was started on Keppra 1000 mg BID for seizure prophylaxis. She will follow up with neurology within one week.

The patient's diagnosis was HAP and possible recurrent C. diff. He underwent a CT abdomen, which showed a decompressed gallbladder with a cholecystostomy tube and bibasilar atelectasis. He was treated with broad-spectrum antimicrobials, including flagyl, zosyn, and vancomycin, and was eventually discharged on levofloxacin and flagyl.

The patient's medications at discharge included levofloxacin, flagyl, and warfarin. He was also prescribed a lovenox bridge and was instructed to continue aggressive pulmonary toilet management, including out-of-bed-to-chair exercises and chest physiotherapy.

Coronary Anatomy

RCA. No competitive flow was seen in the native CX system.

thought to have arm weakness as angina equivalent. Presented

angiogram which was discontinued due to patient

The patient was advised to follow up with her doctor or nurse practitioner for any concerns and to report any new or worsening symptoms.

The patient is a 72-year-old male who was admitted to the neurology service with a chief complaint of encephalopathy. He has a history of glioblastoma, deep vein thrombosis, and IVC filter placement. His past medical history includes right occipital glioblastoma, craniotomy, radiation, and chemotherapy. He also has a history of renal cell cancer, hypertension, hyperlipidemia, osteoarthritis, PMR, neuralgia, and deep vein thrombosis.

The patient's physical exam revealed that he was making noises intermittently, not following commands, and had a sacral decubitus ulcer stage 1. His vital signs were stable, and he had no fever or signs of infection. His laboratory results showed normal blood counts, electrolyte levels, and liver function tests.

The patient's diagnosis was not specified in the report, but it is likely related to his underlying glioblastoma and encephalopathy. He underwent no surgical or invasive procedures during his hospital stay. At discharge, he was prescribed medications including IV keppra, dilantin, PO vancomycin, and oxacillin, and was advised to have a PEG placement for long-term nutrition.

The patient's social history was not provided in the report. His family history revealed that his mother died after hip surgery, his father died of heart failure, and his brother died of a heart attack at age 60. He has two sisters, one with arthritis and carpal tunnel syndrome, and one with Alzheimer's disease. He also has two daughters and a son, all of whom are healthy.

Brief Hospital Course:
___ yo M with suspected metastitic lung ca presenting with 4 days 
of confusion, s/p fall, found to have SAH and hypercalcemia  
.  
# SAH/AMS: Pt had several days of declining mental status 
leading to a fall. Thus SAH was likely traumatic, there was 
concern for it being ___ mets but intially could not do MRI b/c 
pt could not tolerate during delerium. Neurosurgery consulted in 
ED, and do not feel an acute surgical intervention was 
necessary. Neuro checks were institued.  Because of hx of heavy 
alcholol use there was also concern for possible w/drawal 
symptoms and pt was placed on CIWA but tried to minimize use of 
benzos to avoid exacerbating delerium and eventually able to d/c 
CIWA. Pt was given ativan and zyprexa for agitation. CT of head 
was repeated as pt had a fall from bed but SAH remained 
unchanged. Based on this, neurosurge felt that there still was 
not surgical intevention indicated.  Pt continued to have AMS 
and then spiked a fever, CXR looked like possible PNA/effusion 
and had coarse lung sounds so was started on vanco/zosyn for 
likely obstructive or aspiration PNA. Fever improved as did the 
pt's calcium. Slowly AMS improved. (see below for additional 
details)
.  
# Hypercalcemia/AMS: Hypercalcemia was likely secondary to 
metastatic disease. Was low PTH but PTHrp eventually returned 
positive.  Pt received bisphosphonate at OSH prior to arrive to 
___. Calcitonin and IVF fluids given. Calcium decreased but 
continued to remain elevated. Lasix, steroids and additional 
dose of pamidronate were also given. Renal was consulted for 
help with managment and medications were able to be stopped. 
Eventually combination of interventions resulted in 
normalization of calcium. However, later in hospital course 
___, calcium increased again which was able to be 
controlled w/lasix, fluids; at this point was also due for 
another dose of pamidornate which he received on ___. 
Continued to monitor and give fluids and lasix as needed.  
Calcium began rising again and pt was redosed w/pamidronated on 
___ and Ca improved w/IVF and lasix.
.  
# R basilar and RUL lung opacity, PNA: On initial presentation, 
there was a R basilar and RUL lung opacity and could not rule 
out superimposed PNA on CXR. Pt was reported as having reported 
chills, "shaking" (?rigors), and increased cough at home and had 
recent "head cold." Was afebrile on admission. Received one dose 
of levaquin in the ED. Has remained afebrile but had mild 
leukocytosis however pt was on steriods b/c of aggressive 
attempts to treat hypercalcemia. However, pt spiked overnight on 
___ w/worsening respiratory status so was put on 
vanco+zosyn while cultures pending were pending. There was 
concern for likely obstructive PNA but b/c of delerium there was 
also concern for aspiration PNA. Pt's responded well to abx, 
respiratory status improved and eventually mental status 
improved as well. On ___ switched to PO levoquin w/plan for 
additional 14 days (last day on ___. Sputum and blood 
cultures-->NGTD. Pt completed the course of levo which was then 
stopped; however, several days later pt became 
confused/delerious again on ___ and had fever, coarse lung 
sounds w/worsened CXR so restarted levoquin and flagyl to be 
continued and completed at rehab on ___.
.  
# Hypernatremia: likely related to poor PO intake and 
hypercalcemia. IVF switched prn to ___ to help decrease 
sodium, fix free water defecit. Resolved on ___ but on ___ 
had elevated sodium again.  Gave ___ or D5W prn; lytes 
normalized.  
.  
# Hypokalemia: diuresis related. Pt's K was very sensitive to 
administration of 10mg IV lasix; drop to 2.7 after 1 dose. 
Monitored and repleted IV and PO prn; repeated EKG which was wnl 
and kept pt on tele.
.  
# Metastatic lung cancer: Initially difficult to work up given 
AMS. Could not get MRI of head initially b/c pt wouldn't 
tolerate due to agitation and delerium. CT of head was done to 
assess for brain mets as a ___ best study (did not show any 
brain mets but showed known bony met in orbit). As AMS cleared, 
were able to get biopsy which showed NSC lung cancer (squamous). 
Later MRI of brain was obtained which did not show brain mets. 
Given that pt's AMS and clinical status had improved 
substantially, decision was made to try an attenuated course of 
chemo w/Carboplatin and Taxol. Got first course as inpt which pt 
tolerated well w/only mild nausea; was dosed again on ___ 
___. Outpt followup planned w/Dr. ___. Pt will have 
radiation treatment in ___ at ___ 
___. 
.  
# ETOH abuse: Pt has reported heavy history of daily ETOH use. 
Unclear day of last drink. Did not want to give benzos 
unecessarily in state of mental confusion. Pt placed on CIWA to 
assess scoring, watch vitals closely and institute meds only

Output:
answer:
The patient is a 65-year-old male who was admitted to the hospital for management of hypercalcemia. He presented with increased confusion, s/p fall with headstrike, and was found to have a serum calcium level of 17. He has a history of presumed metastatic lung cancer, diagnosed approximately two weeks ago based on imaging studies. He has also had a productive cough for the past year, worsening in the past four days, and has been taking Vicodin at home for pain control.

The patient's past medical history includes a presentation to the emergency department two weeks ago with right-sided chest pain, which was found to be a rib fracture, and he was referred to an oncologist. He was told he likely had metastatic lung cancer and was sent for further testing.

The patient's physical exam revealed a cachetic male with altered mental status, diffuse inspiratory crackles, and poor inspiratory effort. His laboratory results showed elevated white blood cell count, neutrophil count, and lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) levels, as well as elevated calcium and phosphorus levels.

The patient was given 2L of normal saline and IV Levaquin for possible pneumonia, and was also given zoledronic acid. He was transferred to the hospital's emergency department for further management.

Brief Hospital Course:
This is a ___ year old male with past medical history of diabetes 
type 2 uncontrolled complicated by neuropathy, Crohn's disease 
s/p ileocecectomy and multiple laparotomies for SBO, chronic 
diarrhea and recurrent pseudo-obstructions prompting venting 
G-Jtube, admitted ___ w diarrhea and abdominal distention 
thought to represent partial small bowel obstruction, with long 
hospital course course notable for intermittent intolerance of 
tube feeds, subsequently requiring initiation of TPN after long 
discussions with patient and GI service, now tolerating well, 
having regular bowel movements without signs of obstruction or 
diarrhea, discharged home with TPN; 

ACTIVE ISSUES
# Intermittent Small Bowel Obstructions / Diarrhea / Abdominal 
pain / Crohn's Disease / Small Intestine Bacterial Overgrowth - 
On presentation, the patient had acutely worsened diffuse 
chronic abdominal pain on presentation. Subsequent workup 
supported multifactorial causes including likely intermittent 
partial small bowel obstructions, osmotic diarrhea from dietary 
indiscretions (eating lots of starburst candies) and Crohn's 
disease.  Initial concern was for SBO. He had a CT with PO and 
IV contrast that showed mild ileus. However, there was some 
thickening of the bowel wall that initially raised concern for 
Crohn's flare (on subsequent revised read, the thickening was 
deemed artifact). Subsequently, he had a colonoscopy that showed 
ulceration at his colonic-colonic anastomosis with biopsies 
showing terminal ileum inflammation. Given that the results were 
suggestive of Crohn's, the patient was started on budesonide 9mg 
PO daily. Furthermore, in working up his diarrhea, which 
improved with no oral intake, he was found to have a stool Osm 
gap of 357-2*(70+24.3) = 168, which suggested an osmotic 
diarrhea. When tube feed was initiated (see below), his diarrhea 
worsened to over 10 times overnight. Eventually, given that he 
had a borderline SIBO breath test in the past, he was started on 
rifaximin empirically for SIBO treatment. When he started TPN 
(see below), his diarrhea improved to 4 BMs per day.

# Acute Protein Calorie Malnutrition - Patient with > 20lb weigh 
loss over last 6 months.  He had intermittent symptoms as above, 
but failed trials of starting on tube feed on 4 separate 
attempts due to abdominal pain, distension and diarrhea.  He was 
tried on several formulations without improvement. Patient 
educated on risks/benefits of TPN.  Ultimately, he was started 
on TPN via R PICC access. He will eventually need consideration 
for a Port-A-Cath if he is to continue TPN long term--given the 
uncertainty about how long he would require TPN, port not placed 
this admission.  He tolerated TPN well with no electrolyte 
abnormalities and signs of refeeding syndrome. He was giving 
thiamine intermittently throughout his hospitalization with 
tubefeed and TPN. 

# Dysphagia | Esophageal Spasm - The patient reported 
significant swallowing issues during his stay and refused to 
take in POs, including pills. He had a barium swallow study that 
showed findings consistent with diffuse esophageal spasm. GI 
consult team wanted to follow up with manometry testing as 
outpatient before starting therapy. Fortunately, his symptoms 
improved during the course of his stay, and he requested 
trialing PO food and was able to tolerate small amount of POs on 
discharge.

# Chronic Back Pain | Chronic Abdominal Pain - Due to NPO 
status, he was given IV hydromorphone in place of his PO regimen 
at home for the most part of his stay. The patient requested on 
multiple occasions to speak with the Pain specialists here. The 
Pain Consult team saw the patient and recommended uptitration of 
gabapentin to 900mg TID for chronic pain and maintaining his 
home narcotics regimen. The team discussed with the patient that 
his abdominal pain can possibly be attributed to his high 
narcotics requirement leading to narcotics bowel syndrome. The 
patient was very reluctant to the idea of decreasing his pain 
medications, stating that without them, he cannot have a normal 
life and wants to have a life. He was discharged on his home 
regimen, but with the understanding that if abdominal symptoms 
should recur or persist, he may need to consider downtitration 
of opiates. He reported needing more hydromorphone despite 
having filled a recent prescription for a 1 month supply only 2 
weeks prior to admission.

CHRONIC ISSUES
# Hypertension: The patient's BPs had been well controlled 
throughout his admission up until the last part of his stay, 
when he had elevated BPs to 180s at times. His home labetalol 
regimen was uptitrated to 400mg TID from 300mg TID. It's 
possible this is due to switching from IV hydromorphone to PO.

# COPD: No symptoms while hospitalized. He continued home 
inhaler regimen. 

# Diabetes Mellitus: Complicated by neuropathy. Patient endorsed 
neuropathic pain in his feet. He continue home Gabapentin liquid 
as tolerated.

# Coronary Artery Disease: He had no symptoms. He continued ASA 
and labetalol. Lisinopril was DC'ed as outpatient due to labile 
blood pressures.
  
# Anxiety/Depression: The patient's overall mood was stable but 
he became tearful at times when discussing his chronic illness. 
He continue home lorazepam, buproprion, and buspirone.

TRANSITIONAL ISSUES
- TPN: Will need ongoing evaluation of his nutritional 
requirement.
- Access: Patient currently has a PICC for TPN administration. 
If he will be on TPN for a long time, please consider placing a 
Port for longer term access.
- Antibiotics: Will need to continue taking rafaximin 550mg PO 
TID for 7 days, until ___.
- Pain: Patient has been having ongoing issues with abdominal 
pain. His gabapentin was uptitrated to 900mg TID per the 
recommendations of Pain consult team.
- Hypertension: Patient's BP was low earlier during stay but 
became elevated towards the end (160s), likely due to no longer 
receiving IV pain medication. His labetalol was uptitrated to 
400mg TID. Please consider another antihypertensive agent since 
his heart rate is only ___ at discharge.

Output:
answer:
The patient is a 58-year-old male who was admitted to the hospital with a chief complaint of weight loss. He has a history of Crohn's disease, multiple laparotomies, and short gut syndrome, which has led to complications such as intermittent small bowel obstruction and dependence on total parenteral nutrition (TPN). He also has a history of chronic abdominal pain, depression, and anxiety. On admission, his physical examination revealed a diffusely tender abdomen with mild rebound tenderness, and his laboratory results showed elevated white blood cell count, neutrophil count, and C-reactive protein. He was diagnosed with a possible small bowel obstruction and was treated with nebulizer therapy and oral steroids. At discharge, his vital signs were stable, and his physical examination revealed a mild firmness and diffusely tender abdomen. His laboratory results showed improvement in his white blood cell count and C-reactive protein, but his albumin and calcium levels remained low. He was prescribed medications for his chronic pain and was advised to follow up with his primary care physician.

Note: The patient's allergies and adverse reactions are listed as codeine and penicillins.

Discharge Instructions:
Division of Vascular and Endovascular Surgery

Lower Extremity Angiogram Discharge Instructions

Medications:
If instructed, take Aspirin once daily 
If instructed, take Plavix daily
Continue all other medications you were taking before surgery, 
unless otherwise directed
You make take Tylenol or prescribed pain medications for any 
post procedure pain or discomfort

What to expect when you go home:
It is normal to have slight swelling of the legs:
Elevate your leg above the level of your heart (use ___ 
pillows or a recliner) every ___ hours throughout the day and at 
night
Avoid prolonged periods of standing or sitting without your 
legs elevated
It is normal to feel tired and have a decreased appetite, your 
appetite will return with time 
Drink plenty of fluids and eat small frequent meals
It is important to eat nutritious food options (high fiber, 
lean meats, vegetables/fruits, low fat, low cholesterol) to 
maintain your strength and assist in wound healing
To avoid constipation: eat a high fiber diet and use stool 
softener while taking pain medication

What activities you can and cannot do:
When you go home, you may walk and go up and down stairs
You may shower (let the soapy water run over groin incision, 
rinse and pat dry)
Your incision may be left uncovered, unless you have small 
amounts of drainage from the wound, then place a dry dressing or 
band aid over the area that is draining, as needed
No heavy lifting, pushing or pulling (greater than 5 lbs) for 
1 week (to allow groin puncture to heal)
After 1 week, you may resume sexual activity
After 1 week, gradually increase your activities and distance 
walked as you can tolerate
No driving until you are no longer taking pain medications
___ and schedule an appointment to be seen in ___ weeks for 
post procedure check and ultrasound 

What to report to office:
Numbness, coldness or pain in lower extremities 
Temperature greater than 101.5F for 24 hours
New or increased drainage from incision or white, yellow or 
green drainage from incisions
Bleeding from groin puncture site

SUDDEN, SEVERE BLEEDING OR SWELLING (Groin puncture site)
Lie down, keep leg straight and have someone apply firm 
pressure to area for 10 minutes. If bleeding stops, ___ 
vascular office ___. If bleeding does not stop, ___ 
___ for transfer to closest Emergency Room. 

Wet to dry dressing changes

What to Expect at Home

Your doctor or nurse may ask you to change your dressing at 
home. By placing a wet (or moist) gauze dressing in your wound 
and allowing it to dry, wound drainage and dead tissue can be 
removed when you take off the old dressing. 

As the wound heals, you should not need as much gauze (or 
packing gauze). 

Removing the Old Dressing

Follow these steps to remove your dressing: 

Wash your hands. 
Put on a pair of non-sterile gloves. 
Carefully remove the tape. 
Remove the old dressing. If it is sticking to your skin, wet it 
with warm water to loosen it. 
Remove the gauze pads or packing tape from inside your wound. 
Put the old dressing, packing material, and your gloves in a 
plastic bag. Set the bag aside. 
Wash your hands again
Cleaning Your Wound

Follow these steps to clean your wound: 

Put on a new pair of non-sterile gloves. 

Use a soft washcloth to gently clean your wound with warm water 
and soap. Your wound should not bleed much when you are cleaning 
it, but a small amount of blood is okay. Rinse your wound with 
water, and gently pat it dry with a clean towel. Do not rub it 
dry. 

Check the wound for 

Increased redness or swelling 
A bad odor 
Drainage that is darker or thicker
After cleaning your wound, remove your gloves and put them in 
the plastic bag with the old dressing and gloves. 
Wash your hands again.

Changing Your Dressing

Follow these steps to put a new dressing on: 

Put on a new pair of non-sterile gloves. 
Pour saline into a clean bowl. Place gauze pads and any packing 
tape you will use in the bowl. 
Squeeze the saline from the gauze pads or packing tape until it 
is no longer dripping. 
Place the gauze pads or packing tape in your wound. 
Carefully fill in the wound and any spaces under the skin. 
Do not let the wet gauze or packing tape touch any healed skin.
Cover the wet gauze or packing tape with a large dry dressing 
pad. Use tape or rolled gauze to hold this dressing in place. 
Put all used supplies in the plastic bag. Close it securely, 
then put it in a second plastic bag, and close that bag 
securely. Put it in the trash. 

Wash your hands again when you are finished.

When to ___ the Doctor

___ your doctor if you have any of these changes around your 
incision: 

More redness 
More pain 
Swelling 
Bleeding 
The wound is larger or deeper 
The wound looks dried out or dark
Also ___ your doctor if: 

Your temperature is above 100 °F for more than 4 hours 
Drainage is coming from or around the incision or wound and it: 
Is not decreasing after 3 to 5 days 
Is increasing 
Becomes thick, tan, or yellow, or smells bad

___

The patient is a 63-year-old male with a chief complaint of a right foot heel ulcer. He has a history of diabetes type 1, coronary artery disease, peripheral neuropathy, osteoarthritis, and gait disturbance. He was admitted for an angiogram with possible angioplasty and stenting. The patient underwent ultrasound-guided puncture of the left common femoral artery, contralateral second-order catheterization of the right external iliac artery, abdominal aortogram, and serial arteriogram of the right lower extremity. The patient's wound culture showed mixed bacterial types, including Pseudomonas aeruginosa, Staphylococcus aureus, and beta strep.

The patient's medications on admission included atenolol, plavix, furosemide, gabapentin, Lantus, Lispro/Humalog SSI, lisinopril, and simvastatin. At discharge, the patient was prescribed oxycodone-acetaminophen for pain, simvastatin, atenolol, clopidogrel, Lasix, gabapentin, aspirin, Lantus, Humalog, and dicloxacillin.

The patient's discharge diagnosis was a right heel ulcer and diabetic foot infection, as well as diabetes mellitus type 1, angina, coronary artery disease, peripheral neuropathy, osteoarthritis, and gait disturbance. The patient was advised to follow a specific regimen for wound care, including wet to dry dressing changes, and to report any signs of infection or complications to the office.

The patient is a 73-year-old female with a history of non-ischemic cardiomyopathy, chronic systolic heart failure, and chronic kidney disease (CKD). She was admitted for worsening heart failure symptoms, including shortness of breath, orthopnea, and dyspnea. The patient had a history of breast cancer, chemotherapy, and implantation of a biventricular pacemaker/implantable cardioverter-defibrillator (ICD) with cardiac resynchronization therapy (CRT). She was also diagnosed with atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response (RVR) and was started on apixaban, amiodarone, and metoprolol.

The patient underwent a thoracentesis for a large left pleural effusion and was treated for a systolic heart failure exacerbation. She became hypotensive and had an elevated lactate briefly after diuresis and thoracentesis, but responded well to IV fluids. The patient was discharged on the following medications: torsemide, lisinopril, metoprolol succinate, and amiodarone. She was also advised to continue her home regimen of apixaban.

The patient's allergies include codeine, thiazides, ondansetron, digoxin, doxepin, desipramine, sulfa, and adhesive tape. Her past medical history includes hyperlipidemia, cardiomyopathy post chemotherapy, primary prevention ICD implant, CRT defibrillator, and CABG.

INSTRUCTIONS AFTER ORTHOPAEDIC SURGERY:

- You were in the hospital for orthopedic surgery. It is normal 
to feel tired or "washed out" after surgery, and this feeling 
should improve over the first few days to week. 
- Resume your regular activities as tolerated, but please follow 
your weight bearing precautions strictly at all times.

ACTIVITY AND WEIGHT BEARING:
- weight bearing as tolerated on right lower extremity

MEDICATIONS:
- Please take all medications as prescribed by your physicians 
at discharge.
- Continue all home medications unless specifically instructed 
to stop by your surgeon.
- Do not drink alcohol, drive a motor vehicle, or operate 
machinery while taking narcotic pain relievers.
- Narcotic pain relievers can cause constipation, so you should 
drink eight 8oz glasses of water daily and take a stool softener 
(colace) to prevent this side effect.

ANTICOAGULATION:
- Please take subcutaneous heparin daily for 4 weeks

WOUND CARE:
- You may shower. No baths or swimming for at least 4 weeks.
- Any stitches or staples that need to be removed will be taken 
out at your 2-week follow up appointment.
- Please remain in your dressing and do not change unless it is 
visibly soaked or falling off.

DANGER SIGNS:
Please call your PCP or surgeon's office and/or return to the 
emergency department if you experience any of the following:
- Increasing pain that is not controlled with pain medications
- Increasing redness, swelling, drainage, or other concerning 
changes in your incision
- Persistent or increasing numbness, tingling, or loss of 
sensation
- Fever > 101.4
- Shaking chills
- Chest pain
- Shortness of breath
- Nausea or vomiting with an inability to keep food, liquid, 
medications down
- Any other medical concerns

BRIEF HOSPITAL COURSE
=======================
Ms. ___ is a ___ y/o lady with history of alcohol 
abuse who initially presented with coagulopathy, anemia, 
thrombocytopenia, muscosal bleeding, new onset seizure, 
hypotension s/p cardiac arrest with associated respiratory 
failure. She was found to have a large GE junction variceal 
bleed which was stopped with emolization of gastric artery, 
subsequent TIPS. TIPS was complicated by subsequent hepatic 
artery bleed, which caused severe abdominal compartment syndrome 
requiring repeat ___ intervention with embolization of 2 branches 
of hepatic artery.  She was ultimately transitioned to the ICU 
requiring intubation for pending respiratory failure in the 
setting of worsening liver failure (MELD 56), hepatorenal 
syndrome, anasarca, sepsis, and ongoing bleeding.  She was 
eventually made CMO by her family and expired shortly after 
terminal extubation.

# Massive variceal bleed complicated by hepatic arterial bleed - 
Pt presented with massive bleeding from all mucosal surfaces on 
presentation.  Her lower and upper GI tracts prompted an upper 
endoscopy which showed a massive esophageal bleed.  A stat CTA 
showed active extravasation from the GE junction, and she then 
proceeded to ___ for embolization of the gastric artery.  After 
the procedure, she continued to have significant bleeding with 
unstable blood counts and hemodynamics.  She was then referred 
for urgent TIPS procedure which proceeded without complication.  
However, over the next ___ hours her abdomen became massively 
distended and was sent down for a repeat CTA which confirmed 
massive hemoperitoneum as well as probable bleed from the liver. 
 She then went to ___ for a third procedure where they embolized 
2 branches of the hepatic artery.  They also attempted 
paracentesis but did not get substantial drainage has she was 
thought to be clotting off.  However, they did place an ostomy 
bag over the area.  Fortunately, after this final procedure, the 
patient stopped bleeding and was able to continue without blood 
products.  During this course, she received over 29 units of 
blood (massive transfusion protocol utilized), multiple units of 
FFP, platelets, and cryoprecipitate.  The family was informed on 
multiple occasions of her poor prognosis.  She was later 
transitioned to CMO with terminal extubation per discussion with 
her family after her liver function worsened and was complicated 
by HRS.  

# Elevated transaminases with direct hyperbilirubinemia: 
Patient's LFTs started climbing on HD 4 after her bleeding 
stopped.  A RUS was obtained which showed TIPS is patent and no 
dilatation.  Transaminitis and elevated hyperbilirubinemia was 
thought to be due to cholestasis of sepsis versus EtOH 
hepatitis. MELD score elevated to the ___.

#Abdominal Compartment Syndrome- Patient's bladder pressure 
rose, peak pressures rose, and O2 sats declined along with 
massive abdominal distention in the setting of her hemorrhagic 
ascites when she bled from her hepatic artery.  She was taken 
for hepatic artery embolization as described above.  Multiple 
attempts at paracentesis did not yield fluid.  She had repeat 
abdominal ultrasounds that confirmed large amounts of clot in 
the peritoneum.  She developed oliguric renal failure not 
responsive to diuresis.

#Acute Hypoxemic and hypercarbic respiratory Failure: Intubated 
in the setting of new seizure for airway protection.  She 
appeared to have aspirated blood given BRB out of ET tube.  Her 
ventilator settings were quite minimal until she developed 
worsening mechanics ___ abdominal compartment syndrome on ___ 
necessitating increasing O2 requirement and hypercarbia.  After 
the compartment syndrome resolved, she was easily extubated.  
However, the patient was again intubated for metabolic acidosis 
and altered mental status (likely hepatic encephalopathy).  Upon 
discussion with her family regarding her poor prognosis, she was 
terminally extubated after being transitioned to CMO.  She 
expired shortly after extubation. 

# Septic and hypovolemic shock s/p PEA arrest. (Resolved). Echo 
did not show cardiogenic component.  She was on pressors for the 
first few days for which she had a trauma line placed in R IJ.  
Most of the shock was likely hypovolemic from massive bleeding, 
although she did grow MSSA from her sputum as above.  Pressors 
were d/c on ___.  She was initially treated with 
vanc/cefepime/acyclovir and also received a dose of doxycycline 
and clindamycin early on (empiric coverage for tick born disease 
and toxic shock syndrome).  She was eventually narrowed down to 
just cefepime when meningitis was thought to be less likely.  
Blood cultures were ngtd. She later developed significant 
diarrhea, and although her C. diff was negative, she was treated 
with PO vanc/flagyl for presumed C. diff infection.   

#Hepatorenal syndrome: Spun urine is mostly bland, few casts, 1 
muddy brown cast c/w ATN.  Improved with hemodynamic 
stabilization initially.  However, patient developed worsening 
renal failure in the setting of her concurrent liver failure, 
consistent with HRS.  Renal function worsened with albumin 
infusions.  Renal was consulted and because of her poor 
prognosis and unlikely irreversible liver and renal function, 
dialysis was not an option.

#New onset Seizures: Head CT x 2 negative for bleed.  Likely 
secondary to either alcohol withdrawal, toxic-metabolic, or 
meningitis.  Her bleeding and hemodynamic issues precluded 
neuro's initial recommendations of MRI/MRA and LP.  24 hour EEG 
showed focal slowing left side but no seizure concerning for 
structural abnormality.  She was initially treated for 
meningitis as above, but therapy de-escalated when clinically 
appeared unlikely.  MRI/MRA obtained which were overall 
unremarkable for cause of seizure.  She was treated with keppra 
1000 mg BID per neuro recommendations. 

# MSSA PNA/VAP: Sputum sample obtained which grew coag pos 
staph.  She was treated with cefepime for an 8 day course.

answer: The patient is a 52-year-old female who was admitted to the hospital with hypoxia and headaches. She has a history of severe COPD, pseudotumor cerebri, and myocardial infarction with three stents placed. She is also morbidly obese and has diabetes mellitus type 2. The patient's allergies include penicillins, OxyContin, Vicodin, and MS. She was admitted to the MICU for oxygen monitoring and treatment of her hypoxia and headaches. During her admission, she underwent an interventional radiology-guided lumbar puncture and was treated with nebulizers, steroids, and antibiotics. The patient's oxygen saturation improved, and she was eventually transferred to the floor for further treatment. At discharge, she was prescribed medications including acetazolamide, oxycodone, and tylenol, and was scheduled for follow-up appointments with a pulmonologist and sleep medicine specialist.

answer:
The patient is a 43-year-old male with a chief complaint of altered mental status. He has a history of alcoholic cirrhosis, diagnosed with portal vein thrombosis, severe portal hypertension, and gastropathy. He has also had seizures from ethanol withdrawal and has completed a liver transplant workup. The patient's wife reports that his mental status has been worsening over the past three days, and he has been somnolent for the past two days. He was recently discharged from the hospital for hepatic encephalopathy but did not follow up with his clinic appointment and stopped taking his medications.

The patient was admitted to the hospital with a clogged nasogastric tube and was found to have altered mental status, ascites, and a low platelet count. He received a transfusion of 2 PRBCs and was diagnosed with encephalopathy. The patient's laboratory results showed elevated liver enzymes, low albumin levels, and abnormal coagulation studies.

The patient underwent a chest X-ray, which showed a feeding tube ending close to the pylorus, and a liver ultrasound, which showed a shrunken nodular liver appearance consistent with cirrhosis. The patient's medications at discharge included paracentesis every 1-2 weeks and treatment for his encephalopathy.

The patient's allergies and adverse reactions are not specified in the report.

The patient is a 69-year-old male who was admitted to the hospital for surgical management of his coronary artery disease. He presented with chest pain and was referred to Dr. ___ after a stress test. The patient has a history of diabetes type II, dyslipidemia, and GERD. He also has a bullet wound in his left leg from his military service, but there is no retained metal in his body.

The patient underwent coronary artery bypass grafting to three vessels, and the procedure was successful. He was extubated on the first post-operative day and was able to ambulate freely by the time of discharge. The patient was prescribed several medications at discharge, including acetaminophen, furosemide, isosorbide mononitrate, metoprolol tartrate, potassium chloride, rivaroxaban, tamsulosin, tramadol, metformin XR, ascorbic acid, aspirin, fenofibrate nanocrystallized, and glimepiride.

The patient's allergies and adverse reactions include no known allergies or adverse drug reactions. His diagnosis is coronary artery disease, and he underwent cardiac catheterization and TEE (transesophageal echocardiogram) as part of his evaluation.

Output:
answer:
The patient is a 75-year-old female who was admitted for a known large hepatic cyst with progressive shortness of breath over the past year. She has a history of hypertension, hyperlipidemia, urinary incontinence, spinal fusion, ovarian cysts, and osteoporosis. She is allergic to Cipro and Macrodantin.

The patient underwent a laparoscopic converted to open hepatic cyst unroofing, cholecystectomy, and repair of diaphragm with Gore-Tex patch, as well as ERCP. The cyst was benign and contained approximately 3 liters of clear fluid. The patient developed a bile leak and pancreatitis post-operatively, which was managed with a biliary stent and conservative treatment.

The patient was prescribed several medications at discharge, including acetaminophen, oxycodone, atenolol, simvastatin, fluticasone-salmeterol, docusate sodium, solifenacin, alendronate, calcium, and multivitamin. She was also advised to follow up with her doctor and to monitor her incision for redness, drainage, or bleeding.

The patient was discharged home with a JP drain still in place and was advised to follow up with her doctor for further treatment and management of her condition.

Discharge Instructions:
INSTRUCTIONS AFTER ORTHOPAEDIC SURGERY:

- You were in the hospital for orthopedic surgery. It is normal 
to feel tired or "washed out" after surgery, and this feeling 
should improve over the first few days to week. 
- Resume your regular activities as tolerated, but please follow 
your weight bearing precautions strictly at all times.

ACTIVITY AND WEIGHT BEARING:
-Touchdown weightbearing left lower extremity

MEDICATIONS:
  1)  Take Tylenol ___ every 6 hours around the clock. This is 
an over the counter medication.
  2)  Add oxycodone as needed for increased pain. Aim to wean 
off this medication in 1 week or sooner.  This is an example on 
how to wean down:
Take 1 tablet every 3 hours as needed x 1 day,
then 1 tablet every 4 hours as needed x 1 day,
then 1 tablet every 6 hours as needed x 1 day,
then 1 tablet every 8 hours as needed x 2 days, 
then 1 tablet every 12 hours as needed x 1 day,
then 1 tablet every before bedtime as needed x 1 day.  
Then continue with Tylenol for pain.
  3)  Do not stop the Tylenol until you are off of the narcotic

contact the office to set up an appointment because we cannot 
refill this type of pain medication over the phone. 
  5)  Narcotic pain relievers can cause constipation, so you 
should drink eight 8oz glasses of water daily and continue 
following the bowel regimen as stated on your medication 
prescription list. These meds (senna, colace, miralax) are over 
the counter and may be obtained at any pharmacy.

machinery while taking narcotic pain relievers.

physicians at discharge.

instructed to stop by your surgeon.

The patient is a 74-year-old male who was admitted to the hospital due to a left TFN cutout with symptomaticity. He presented with left hip pain and was unable to bear weight. The patient has a history of CAD, CABG, AF, hypertension, hyperlipidemia, right carotid endarterectomy, CKD, BPH, low back pain, R knee meniscus tear, colon polyps, osteoporosis, anemia, and scalp melanoma. He has no known allergies or adverse drug reactions.

The patient underwent a major surgical procedure, status post removal of hardware of the left TFN, conversion to left hip hemiarthroplasty. He was given IV fluids and IV pain medications during his hospital stay and was discharged on oral medications, including acetaminophen, bisacodyl, dabigatran, docusate sodium, finasteride, fish oil, pantoprazole, rosuvastatin, senna, tamsulosin, and tramadol.

The patient was discharged in stable condition, with his pain well-controlled, incisions clean and dry, and able to void and move bowels spontaneously. He was instructed to follow up with his surgeon and to continue taking his medications as prescribed.

Brief Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted  and was taken to the operating room 
for a total knee arthroplasty. Please see operative report for

the procedure well. Pain was initially controlled with a PCA

found to be clean and intact without erythema or abnormal

the wound was benign. The patient progressed well with physical 
therapy. Post-operative Xrays demonstrated hardware in good 
position. The patient was discharged in stable condition.  The 
patient's weight-bearing status is weight bearing as tolerated 
on the operative extremity.

Discharge Instructions:
Weigh yourself every morning, call MD if weight goes up more 
than 3 lbs.
1. Please return to the emergency department or notify your 
physician if you experience any of the following: severe pain 
not relieved by medication, increased swelling, decreased 
sensation, difficulty with movement, fevers greater than 101.5, 
shaking chills, increasing redness or drainage from the incision 
site, chest pain, shortness of breath or any other concerns.
2. Please follow up with your primary physician regarding this 
admission and any new medications and refills.
3. Resume your home medications unless otherwise instructed.
4. You have been given medications for pain control. Please do 
not drive, operate heavy machinery, or drink alcohol while 
taking these medications. As your pain decreases, take fewer 
tablets and increase the time between doses. This medication can 
cause constipation, so you should drink plenty of water daily 
and take a stool
softener (such as colace) as needed to prevent this side effect.
5. You may not drive a car until cleared to do so by your 
surgeon or your primary physician.
6. Please keep your wounds clean. You may shower starting five 
days after surgery, but no tub baths or swimming for at least 
four weeks. No dressing is needed if wound continues to be 
non-draining. Any stitches or staples that need to be removed 
will be taken out by the visiting nurse or rehab facility two 
weeks after your surgery.
7. Please call your surgeon's office to schedule or confirm your 
follow-up appointment in four weeks.
8. Please DO NOT take any non-steroidal anti-inflammatory 
medications (NSAIDs such as celebrex, ibuprofen, advil, aleve, 
motrin, etc).
9. ANTICOAGULATION: Please continue your lovenox for three weeks 
to help prevent deep vein thrombosis (blood clots).  After 
completing the lovenox, please take Aspirin 325mg daily for an 
additional three weeks.

answer:
The patient is a 64-year-old male who was admitted to the hospital for a total knee replacement due to right knee pain caused by osteoarthritis and significant flexion contracture. He has a history of obesity, atrial fibrillation, congestive heart failure, COPD/RAD, gastric sleeve surgery, incarcerated ventral hernia repair with mesh, obstructive sleep apnea, chronic venous stasis disease, hypothyroidism, left hip replacement, and right knee repair and replacement. He has allergies to propafenone and warfarin.

The patient underwent a total knee arthroplasty and tolerated the procedure well. He was started on pain medications and was placed in a CPM machine to improve range of motion. He received IV antibiotics and lovenox for DVT prophylaxis.

At discharge, the patient was taking several medications, including aspirin, simvastatin, gabapentin, acetaminophen, ursodiol, levothyroxine, citalopram, trazodone, multivitamin, folic acid, ferrous sulfate, hydromorphone, metoprolol, and diltiazem. He was also prescribed mupirocin calcium ointment and lovenox injections.

The patient was discharged in stable condition, with instructions to follow up with his primary physician and to weigh himself daily. He was advised to return to the emergency department or notify his physician if he experiences any severe pain, increased swelling, decreased sensation, difficulty with movement, fevers, shaking chills, or other concerns.

arterial ischemia at this location. If there is a concern for

.

Brief Hospital Course:
___ yo man with history of AML s/p allo-BMT ___ who presents 
with tender cutaneous lesions and polycythemia.  He had 
presented to clinic with splinter hemorrhages but had negative 
blood cultures and TTE on ___.  He also had polycythemia with 
an elevated erythropoeitin level.  
His fingers became painful and he was admitted for an expedited 
workup of splinter hemorrhages.  On admission his finger (left 
index finger) was slightly discolored.  He had blood cultures 
that were negative.  He has a TTE that was negative and then a 
TEE, which he tolerated well.  Both tests showed no signs of 
vegetations on the valves or thrombi.  It was felt that 
endocarditis as a cause of his finger lesions (splinter 
hemorrhages) was unlikely in the face of this data.  He also did 
not have these lesions else where.  A differential including 
GVHD vasculitis or rheumatological vasculitidies was formulated 
and a rheumatology consult was called.  Extensive serologies 
were sent, and his ___ was positive with a titer of 1:320.  This 
was felt to be non-specific.  A hypercoagulability panel was 
sent with negative results.  He also had negative cryoglobulins. 
 Rheumatology had suggested that he be evaluated by dermatology 
for his skin changes (distal digits had started becoming 
slightly purple-blue).  Vascular surgery had also evaluated him. 
 They felt that this was not a surgical issue as he had 
dopplerable pulses to the mid-phalanx.  They also felt that an 
MRA or CTA of his arms would not be helpful as he had 
dopplerable pulses and likely did not have a thrombi occluding 
his vessels.  He had an ultrasound of his arteries in the upper 
extremities which was normal.  He was started on Solumedrol 40mg 
a day. Dermatology had suggested that there may be a 
vaso-spastic element to his finger presentation. He was started 
on Nifedipine 10mg po bid and NTG oitment to his fingertips.  
This was somewhat helpful with his finger pain.  A CT-A of his 
right arm was performed that showed atherosclerosis of his ulnar 
and radial artery, no evidence of vasculitis was seen.  
Cholesterol levels were obtained for risk stratification.   He 
had an acute worsening  of the color of his fingertips (to a 
more intense purple-blue) with maintenance of his pulses.  His 
solumedrol dose was increased to 250mg q 6 hours and his 
cellcept was increased to 500mg po tid.  Dermatology was spoken 
with and they performed a skin biopsy before the higher dose 
solumedrol and cellcept was initiated. In addition a heparin gtt 
was initiated.  One day following the 1g per day solumedrol and 
anticoagulation, his fingertips improved in pain and pallor.  
The results of the biopsy showed one arteriole with a waxy 
deposit consistent with cyroglobulins and one thrombus in an 
arteriole.  No vasculitis was seen.  
Repeat cryoglobulins were sent, and were hand delivered in a 
warm water bath to ensure that they did not precipitate en 
route.  The results were negative.  
After 3 days on anticoagulation it was stopped as it was decided 
that the risk outweighed the benefit (as he is unsteady 
secondary to his cerebral palsy)and the indication for 
anticoagulation was not present.  His fingers continued to be 
improved.  The solumedrol was gradually tapered to prednisone 
40mg a day and eventually to 30mg a day prior to discharge.  On 
___ he received Rituxan as it has been used in 
cryoglobulinemia.  He tolerated it well.  At the time of 
discharge his finger lesions were felt to be due to a 
vasculopathy with a vasospastic element. 
He had an increase in lower extremity edema that was felt to be 
secondary to the high dose steroids but a lower extremity 
ultrasound was done to assess for DVTs.  This exam was negative. 
  
His blood sugars were quite elevated after the initiation of 
high dose steroids.  He was followed by the ___ clinic.  He 
required q 6 hours NPH and a sliding scale.  As his steroids 
were tapered his insulin needs decreased.  He was discharged on 
glipizide and once daily 70/30.
His hematocrit remained high and an epo level was tested again 
to confirm the initial high reading.  The repeat epo was high.

Output:
answer:
The patient is a 52-year-old male with a chief complaint of concern for vasculitis. He has a history of acute myeloid leukemia (AML) and has undergone allogenic stem cell transplant with busulfan and cytoxan for conditioning regimen. He recently developed increasing hematocrit and splinter hemorrhages in his nailbeds. The patient has a past medical history of AML, mild chronic graft-versus-host disease of eyes and mouth, cerebral palsy, psoriasis, and a history of upper extremity deep vein thrombosis. He has no known allergies to drugs.

The patient's diagnosis is erythroid hyperplasia, and he underwent a skin biopsy of the left index finger and a trans-esophageal echocardiogram. The patient's medications at discharge include phlebotomy.

The patient's laboratory results show normal hemoglobin electrophoresis, elevated erythropoietin, and normal blood cultures. His bone marrow biopsy shows erythroid hyperplasia with mildly megaloblastoid maturation. His clot section and biopsy slides show normal myeloid elements and megakaryocytes.

The patient's radiology results show patent bilateral brachial/radial and ulnar arteries with normal waveforms and velocities, and no evidence of deep venous thrombosis in the bilateral lower extremities. His echocardiogram shows minimal aortic valve sclerosis with no vegetations or pathologic flow identified.

Discharge Instructions:
You were admitted to the hospital after a Right Sided Colectomy 
and hernia repair for surgical management of your colon polyp 
and incisional hernia. You have recovered from this procedure 
well and you are now ready to return home. Samples from your 
colon were taken and this tissue has been sent to the pathology 
department for analysis. You will receive these pathology 
results at your follow-up appointment. If there is an urgent 
need for the surgeon to contact you regarding these results they 
will contact you before this time. You have tolerated a regular 
diet, passing gas and your pain is controlled with pain 
medications by mouth. You may return home to finish your 
recovery.  
You will be discharged home on Lovenox injections to prevent 
blood clots after surgery. You will take this for 30 days after 
your surgery date. This will be given once daily. Please follow 
all nursing teaching instruction given by the nursing staff. 
Please monitor for any signs of bleeding: fast heart rate, 
bloody bowel movements, abdominal pain, bruising, feeling faint 
or weak. If you have any of these symptoms please call our 
office for advice or seek medical attention if there is an 
emergency. Avoid any contact activity while taking Lovenox. 
Please take extra caution to avoid falling.

 Please monitor your bowel function closely. You may or may not 
have had a bowel movement prior to your discharge which is 
acceptable, however it is important that you have a bowel 
movement in the next ___ days. After anesthesia it is not 
uncommon for patients to have some decrease in bowel function 
but you should not have prolonged constipation. Some loose stool 
and passing of small amounts of dark, old appearing blood are 
expected. However, if you notice that you are passing bright red 
blood with bowel movements or having loose stool without 
improvement please call the office or go to the emergency room 
if the symptoms are severe. If you are taking narcotic pain 
medications there is a risk that you will have some 
constipation. Please take an over the counter stool softener 
such as Colace, and if the symptoms do not improve call the 
office.  
 If you have any of the following symptoms please call the 
office for advice ___:  
 fever greater than 101.5  
 increasing abdominal distension  
 increasing abdominal pain  
 nausea/vomiting  
 inability to tolerate food or liquids  
 prolonged loose stool  
 extended constipation  
 inability to urinate  
 Incisions:  
 You have a long vertical surgical incisions on your abdomen 
closed with staples. This is healing well however it is 
important that you monitor these areas for signs and symptoms of 
infection including: increasing redness of the incision lines, 
white/green/yellow/malodorous drainage, increased pain at the 
incision, increased warmth of the skin at the incision, or 
swelling of the area.  
 You may shower; pat the incisions dry with a towel, do not rub. 
The small incisions may be left open to the air. If closed with 
steri-strips (little white adhesive strips), these will fall off 
over time, please do not remove them. Please no baths or 
swimming until cleared by the surgical team.  
 Pain  
 It is expected that you will have pain after surgery and this 
pain will gradually improved over the first week or so you are 
home. You will especially have pain when changing positions and 
with movement. You should continue to take 2 Extra Strength 
Tylenol (___) for pain every 8 hours around the clock and you 
may also take Advil (Ibuprofen) 600mg every 6 hours for 7 days. 
Please do not take more than 3000mg of Tylenol in 24 hours or 
any other medications that contain Tylenol such as cold 
medication. Do not drink alcohol while or Tylenol. Please take 
Advil with food. If these medications are not controlling your 
pain to a point where you can ambulate and preform minor tasks, 
you should take a dose of the narcotic pain medication 
oxycodone. Please take this only if needed for pain. Do not take 
with any other sedating medications or alcohol. Do not drive a 
car if taking narcotic pain medications.  
 Activity  
 You may feel weak or "washed out" for up to 6 weeks after 
surgery. No heavy lifting greater than a gallon of milk for 3 
weeks. You may climb stairs. You may go outside and walk, but 
avoid traveling long distances until you speak with your 
surgical team at your first follow-up visit. Your surgical team 
will clear you for heavier exercise and activity as the observe 
your progress at your follow-up appointment. You should only 
drive a car on your own if you are off narcotic pain medications 
and feel as if your reaction time is back to normal so you can 
react appropriately while driving. You will have a physical 
therapist visit you at home to work on your strength and 
mobility.  
 Thank you for allowing us to participate in your care! Our hope 
is that you will have a quick return to your life and usual 
activities. Good luck!

The patient was discharged home with instructions to continue his medications, including insulin, glipizide, lisinopril, metformin, and simvastatin, as well as Lovenox injections to prevent blood clots. He was also advised to follow up with his surgeon in 30 days to receive pathology results.

2. Please follow up with your primary physician regarding this 
admission and any new medications and refills. 

3. Resume your home medications unless otherwise instructed.

4. You have been given medications for pain control. Please do 
not drive, operate heavy machinery, or drink alcohol while 
taking these medications. As your pain decreases, take fewer 
tablets and increase the time between doses. This medication can 
cause constipation, so you should drink plenty of water daily 
and take a stool softener (such as colace) as needed to prevent 
this side effect.  Call your surgeons office 3 days before you 
are out of medication so that it can be refilled.  These 
medications cannot be called into your pharmacy and must be 
picked up in the clinic or mailed to your house.  Please allow 
an extra 2 days if you would like your medication mailed to your

surgeon.

(5) days after surgery, but no tub baths or swimming for at 
least four (4) weeks. No dressing is needed if wound continues 
to be non-draining. Any stitches or staples that need to be 
removed will be taken out at your follow-up visit in three (3) 
weeks after your surgery.

7. Please call your surgeon's office to schedule or confirm your 
follow-up appointment in three (3) weeks.

8. Please DO NOT take any non-steroidal anti-inflammatory 
medications (NSAIDs such as celebrex, ibuprofen, advil, aleve, 
motrin, etc).

weeks to help prevent deep vein thrombosis (blood clots).  If 
you were taking aspirin prior to your surgery, it is OK to 
continue at your previous dose while taking this medication.

okay to shower five days after surgery but no tub baths, 
swimming, or submerging your incision until after your four (4) 
week checkup. Please place a dry sterile dressing on the wound 
each day if there is drainage, otherwise leave it open to air.

thick yellow drainage. Staples will be removed at your follow-up 
visit in three (3) weeks.

that these represented a new seizure type, the patient was 
admitted for longterm continuous video EEG monitoring.

pushbuttons for body twitching that had no EEG correlate, 
however she did not have any of the typical dizzy spells. She

answer: The patient is a 44-year-old female who presents to the neurology service with a chief complaint of dizzy spells. She has a history of refractory epilepsy and a prior right temporal lobectomy due to an oligodendroglioma. She has been experiencing episodes of dizziness, nausea, and vomiting, followed by bilateral leg weakness, which have been occurring for several months. Her past medical history includes epilepsy, atrial fibrillation, iron deficiency anemia, depression, GERD, and ADHD. She has been treated with multiple antiepileptic drugs, including Lamictal, which was tapered off during her hospital stay. The patient's physical examination is normal, with the exception of an irregular heart rhythm. Her neurologic examination reveals normal cognitive function, with no deficits in sensation, motor function, or coordination. The patient's EEG monitoring showed frequent bursts of left temporal theta slowing, but no electrographic seizures. She was admitted for long-term continuous video EEG monitoring to characterize her events and determine the cause of her symptoms.

The patient is a 68-year-old male who was admitted to the hospital with a chief complaint of worsening abdominal pain, fever, and bloody urine. He has a history of cirrhosis, ascites, and portal hypertension, and has undergone several previous hospitalizations for similar symptoms. He is also a heavy drinker and has a history of chronic anemia.

On admission, the patient's vital signs were stable, and his physical examination revealed a diffusely tender abdomen, flank tenderness, and yellow stool in his urostomy and ileal conduit. Laboratory results showed elevated white blood cell count, hemoglobin, and platelet count, as well as abnormal liver function tests.

The patient was treated with antibiotics and diuretics, and his condition improved. He was discharged with a diagnosis of pyelonephritis and was prescribed medications to manage his symptoms.

The patient's allergies include aspirin, Tylenol, and ibuprofen. He has a history of bleeding from his ileal conduit and has undergone several previous procedures, including a TIPS procedure.

The patient's social history is significant for active ethanol abuse, and his family history is significant for colon cancer, diabetes, and hypertension.

The patient's physical examination on discharge revealed a stable vital sign, a soft and diffusely tender abdomen, and a palpable liver. His laboratory results showed improvement in his liver function tests and a normal platelet count.

Overall, the patient's condition is stable, and he is being discharged with a plan for further management of his symptoms.

Output:
answer:
The patient is an 84-year-old female who was admitted with a chief complaint of gastrointestinal bleed. She has a history of hypertension, hyperlipidemia, ischemic cardiomyopathy, and paroxysmal atrial fibrillation. She also has a history of mitral regurgitation and squamous cell carcinoma of the right lung. 

The patient's presenting symptoms included hematemesis, diarrhea, constipation, and fatigue. Her physical examination revealed conjunctival pallor, dark stool, and coffee-ground emesis. Laboratory results showed anemia, thrombocytopenia, and elevated liver enzymes. 

The patient underwent an upper gastrointestinal endoscopy (EGD) which revealed erosions in the gastroesophageal junction, stomach, and duodenum, as well as granularity and erythema in the stomach and duodenum compatible with gastritis and duodenitis. 

The patient was diagnosed with moderate to severe regional systolic dysfunction and moderate to severe mitral regurgitation. She was prescribed high-dose proton pump inhibitors twice a day and was recommended to undergo a repeat EGD in 8 weeks for evaluation of the esophageal erosion.

answer: The patient is a 52-year-old male who was admitted to the hospital with bilateral foot ulcers. He has a history of uncontrolled diabetes type 1 and was brought to the emergency department by his girlfriend due to his inability to seek medical attention due to insurance issues. The patient's presenting symptoms included bilateral foot infections and swelling. He was found to have infected ulcers on both feet, with one ulcer on the left foot requiring incision and drainage. The patient underwent debridement of the right ulcer and excision of the left sesamoids with wound closure. He was started on Lantus and a humalog sliding scale for his diabetes, and was also prescribed antibiotics for his foot infections. The patient was advised to be non-weightbearing on the left foot and full weightbearing on the right foot. He was seen by a podiatrist and cleared for home, with follow-up appointments scheduled.

Brief Hospital Course:
HOSPITAL COURSE: Ms. ___ is a ___ woman with uterine sarcoma who 
presented to the ED with nausea/vomiting and obstipation x 1 
week. A CT of her abdomen showed findings consistent with small 
bowel obstruction, secondary to new pelvic mass from her uterine 
sarcoma, despite recent therapy with gemcitabine/docetaxol, 
which provided temporary symptomatic improvement. She was 
admitted for palliative chemotherapy with adriamycin/ifosfamide, 
and was put on bowel rest with NPO/TPN. She became neutropenic 
after the first round, and responded well to Neupogen. Her 
obstructive symptoms worsened, however, requiring placement of a 
jejunal tube for decompression, leading to relief of nausea and 
vomiting. Repeat abdominal CT showed enlargement of the mass. 
After a family meeting and input from her outpatient oncologist 
Dr. ___ opted for a second cycle of 
adriamycin/ifosfamide. She tolerated it well and was sent home 
on TPN, with ___ services.  

# SMALL BOWEL OBSTRUCTION SECONDARY TO UTERINE SARCOMA: Patient 
presented following vacation in ___ with naueas/vomiting 
and loss of bowel movements. CT abdomen showed a new pelvic 
mass, likely progression of uterine sarcoma. Patient was placed 
on bowel rest and strict NPO until initiated on TPN. A port was 
placed received a surface echo in preparation for chemotherapy. 
Patient was started on adriamycin/iphosphamide  on ___ and 
tolerated the infusions well with negative UAs during treatment. 
Patient was continued on TPN and bowel rest with NGT placed 
following vomiting of feculent material. NGT was pulled 
following nose pain as requested by the patient. She was then 
started on clear liquids with occasional vomiting secondary to 
crampy pain in her abdomen. Gyn oncology was following the 
patient and recommended conservative management. 

After many days of symptom management and persistent nausea, 
vomiting of copious feculent/bloody material, abdominal pain, 
and inability to tolerate clears, a repeat CT was performed 
which showed enlargement of the pelvic mass despite the cycle of 
A/I. A PEJ tube was placed for decompression, which provided 
significant symptom management. Prior to tube placement pt had 
intractable vomiting and had several episodes of tachycardia 
secondary to hypovolemia from poor PO intake, responsive to 
fluid. For this reason her home furosemide was discontinued 
until outpatient follow-up with her PCP.

Gyn/Onc said no surgical options for the mass were available, 
and pt appeared to have failed first cycle of A/I as mass had 
progressed, however the CT was done much earlier than 
anticipated (original plan was to hold off until after second 
round), so it may have been too soon to evaluate effect of 
chemo. Family meeting was held and pt decided, after input from 
Dr. ___ Heme/Onc fellow Dr. ___, as well as inpatient 
oncology team and Palliative Care, that she would like to try 
another round of A/I and hope for better results. Three days of 
treatment were administered, which pt tolerated well other than 
some nausea and vomiting, all UAs were clear. She was discharged 
home on regular diet for comfort, but with TPN and PEJ in place. 
Insulin was added to the nightly TPN the day before discharge 
due to AM blood sugars in the high 100s/low 200s, so pt was sent 
home with glucometer and test strips for ___ self-checks, with 
___ instructions to monitor blood glucose. She got a Neulasta 
shot before discharge. She will be followed as an outpatient by 
Dr. ___.

Discharge Instructions:
Dear Ms. ___,

You were admitted for nausea/vomiting and lack of bowel 
movements. It was found that you had a new tumor that was 
obstructing your small bowel. You were started on nutrition 
through your IV and given bowel rest.  You also received 
chemotherapy while admitted, which you tolerated well.  Your 
blood counts dropped and were supported with neupogen shots.  At 
first, your nausea and vomiting and pain were controlled with IV 
medications.  You eventually tolerated a liquid diet.

Later in your hospital course, your small bowel obstruction 
worsened, an on imaging it appeared as if your tumor had grown, 
which caused a worsening of your symptoms of abdominal pain, 
nausea, and vomiting. We had you go to surgery to get a tube 
placed to relieve these symptoms, and gave you another round of 
chemo to try to shrink the mass enough to give you some relief 
from the obstruction. You tolerated the second round of chemo 
well and your symptoms improved with the chemo and the placement 
of the gastric tube. You will continue getting nutrition through 
your IV at home. 

Your blood sugars have been high occasionally in the hospital 
while on TPN, so insulin was added to your IV nutrition. We will 
send you home with a device you can use to test your blood sugar 
___. Symptoms of low blood sugar are shakiness, nausea, 
sweating, palpitations. If you have any of these symptoms or are 
feeling ill, check your blood sugar, eat something, and call 
your doctor for ___ blood sugar less than 80. For blood sugar 
>250, please also notify your doctor.

___
 

Output:
answer:
The patient is a 50-year-old female who was admitted to the hospital with a chief complaint of nausea and vomiting. She has a history of uterine leiomyosarcoma that has metastasized to her lung and has undergone surgery and adjuvant radiation therapy. She has also received chemotherapy with gemcitabine and docetaxel, but had to stop due to toxicity. She presented to the emergency department with sudden onset abdominal pain and new abdominal masses, and was found to have three new masses on CT scan. She has not had a bowel movement in a week and has not passed gas. She has also experienced episodes of non-bloody emesis.

The patient's past medical history is significant for menopausal symptoms, fibroids, and a history of open cholecystectomy. She has no history of asthma, heart disease, diabetes, hypertension, thromboembolic disease, or breast cancer.

The patient's diagnosis is leiomyosarcoma with metastatic disease to the lung and abdomen. She underwent a total abdominal hysterectomy, bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy, and bilateral lymph node sampling, and was found to have lymphovascular invasion and positive for desmin.

The patient was treated with gemcitabine and docetaxel, but had to stop due to an allergic reaction to docetaxel. She was also given fluids and CT scans to monitor her disease progression.

The patient's physical exam was significant for a tender abdomen, particularly in the left lower quadrant, and a superficial mass in the periumbilical area. Her discharge physical exam was similar, with no significant changes.

The patient's laboratory results showed elevated white blood cell count, neutrophils, and lactate levels, as well as decreased hemoglobin and hematocrit levels. Her electrolyte levels were also abnormal, with elevated sodium and potassium levels.

The patient's relevant studies included a CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast, which showed multiple air and fluid-filled dilated loops of small bowel with necrosis.

Output:
answer:
The patient is a 74-year-old male who was admitted for lower extremity ischemia. He has a history of extensive peripheral vascular disease, including a left common femoral to posterior tibial artery bypass graft, and has undergone several debridements of ulcers on his feet, followed by a below-knee amputation on his right side. He has also had balloon angioplasty of his left common femoral to posterior tibial artery vein graft.

The patient's past medical history includes coronary artery disease, myocardial infarction, coronary artery bypass grafting, diabetes mellitus, hypertension, hypercholesterolemia, chronic renal insufficiency, and anemia.

He has allergies to iodine and iodine-containing substances. His medications at admission included amiodarone, Dubrox, Lunesta, Fluticasone, Gabapentin, Humalin, Vicodin, Imdur, Levoxyl, omeprazole, renegel, sun, zocor, coumadin, Colace, and Senna.

The patient underwent a diagnostic left lower extremity angiogram and was found to have patent left femoral to posterior tibial bypass with mildly elevated velocities at the distal anastomosis, suggesting the possibility of moderate stenosis.

At discharge, the patient's medications included amiodarone, nitroglycerin, Levothyroxine, Gabapentin, Fluticasone, B Complex-Vitamin C-Folic Acid, Carvedilol, Citalopram, Oxycodone-Acetaminophen, Lactulose, Lisinopril, Lorazepam, omeprazole, Sevelamer HCl, Simvastatin, Aspirin, Docusate Sodium, Acetaminophen, insulin, and Coumadin.

The patient was discharged to an extended care facility with instructions to weigh himself daily, take his medications as directed, and follow a diet that includes high fiber, lean meats, vegetables, fruits, low fat, and low cholesterol. He was also advised to elevate his legs above the level of his heart, avoid prolonged periods of standing or sitting, and drink plenty of fluids.

The patient, a 91-year-old male, was admitted to the hospital with a chief complaint of intracerebral hemorrhage (ICH) in the right hemisphere. He was found unresponsive on the floor by his grandson and was taken to the hospital where a head CT scan revealed a large hemorrhage with intraventricular spread. The patient was intubated and started on a propofol drip. He was previously well, living alone and fully independent, with a history of atrial fibrillation, hypertension, and benign prostatic hypertrophy.

The patient's physical examination revealed a critically ill patient with a Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS) of 3, with no response to voice or deep noxious stimuli. His neurologic examination showed anisocoria, with a right pupil that was minimally reactive to light, and a left pupil that was also minimally reactive. He had no movement to noxious stimuli on the left arm and was internally rotating his left leg to nailbed pressure.

The patient's laboratory results showed elevated levels of creatine kinase (CK), troponin T, and lactate, as well as an anion gap and hyperosmolality. His urine analysis showed proteinuria and hematuria.

The patient's hospital course was complicated by respiratory arrest, which led to his death. He was diagnosed with subarachnoid hemorrhage following injury, pneumonia, and other amyloidosis. He was prescribed several medications, including diltiazem, metoprolol, and vancomycin, but expired before discharge.

Note: The patient's medications on admission were aspirin, diltiazem, finasteride, digoxin, metoprolol, and lasix, but he was not prescribed any medications at discharge since he expired.

She has a history of hypertension, diabetes, non-obstructive coronary artery disease, and end-stage renal disease (ESRD) requiring hemodialysis. She was found to be unresponsive and pulseless, but was successfully resuscitated with cardiopulmonary resuscitation (CPR) and defibrillation. She was intubated and sedated, and was started on amiodarone for arrhythmias.

The patient has a history of allergies to penicillins, amoxicillin, clindamycin, and other antibiotics. She has also had a recent viral infection and has been taking various medications, including blood pressure medications, antidiabetic medications, and antihypertensive medications.

The patient's physical examination revealed a sedated and intubated patient with low tone, elevated jugular venous pressure, and bilateral breath sounds. Her laboratory results showed elevated blood glucose, creatinine, and urea nitrogen levels, as well as abnormal liver function tests.

The patient's diagnosis is cardiac arrest due to ventricular tachycardia, and she was treated with amiodarone and other medications. She was also started on a cooling protocol to reduce her body temperature.

The patient's medications at discharge include amiodarone, propofol, and other medications to manage her arrhythmias and other medical conditions.

The patient is an 82-year-old female who was admitted to the hospital with a chief complaint of dyspnea on exertion. She has a history of metastatic breast cancer and is currently taking everolimus and exemestane. She was found to have worsening shortness of breath over the past five days, which was initially attributed to worsening of her metastatic breast cancer. However, further evaluation revealed a 2:1 AV block on ECG, which was likely due to AV node disease. She was admitted to the EP service for placement of a permanent pacemaker.

The patient's past medical history is significant for metastatic ER+ breast cancer, bilateral mastectomy, Adriamycin, tamoxifen, Arimidex, exemestane, Faslodex, XRT to left axillary nodes and subpectoralis nodes, Xeloda, glaucoma, bladder suspension, and cholecystectomy.

The patient has no known allergies, but is allergic to morphine. She has no significant social or family history.

On admission, the patient's physical exam revealed a bradycardic rhythm, bibasilar crackles in the lungs, and no pedal edema. Her discharge physical exam showed a regular rhythm, no pedal edema, and a soft, non-distended abdomen.

The patient's laboratory results showed a lactate level of 1.9, troponin T < 0.01, and proBNP of 4769. Her chest CT scan showed increased left perihilar and lower lung opacities concerning for pneumonia.

The patient was discharged with a diagnosis of AV node disease and was prescribed medications for her condition.
